1→# Curriculum Specification
     2→
     3→**Version:** 2.1
     4→**Last Updated:** 2026-01-19
     5→**Purpose:** Defines the structure, philosophy, and content model for all platform curricula.
     6→
     7→---
     8→
     9→## Overview
    10→
    11→"Code Like It's 198x" teaches retro game development through complete, playable games. Each platform has a sequence of games ordered by complexity. Learners build real games from the first unit, with skills compounding through the sequence.
    12→
    13→### The Problem with Phases and Tiers
    14→
    15→The original curriculum model used:
    16→- 8 phases × 16 tiers × 32 lessons = 4,096 lessons per platform
    17→- Rigid structure that didn't scale across platforms of different complexity
    18→- Abstract lesson progression disconnected from tangible outcomes
    19→
    20→This produced technically correct but lifeless content - lessons that taught concepts in isolation rather than building toward something the learner cared about.
    21→
    22→### The Games Model
    23→
    24→The new model organises learning around complete games:
    25→- Each platform has a sequence of games, ordered by complexity
    26→- Each game contains 8, 16, or 32 units (powers of 2)
    27→- Each unit is 60-120 minutes and produces a working intermediate result
    28→- Skills compound naturally as games increase in sophistication
    29→
    30→Learners always know what they're building and why. The game provides context; the units provide pacing.
    31→
    32→---
    33→
    34→## Unit Structure
    35→
    36→### Unit Count Per Game
    37→
    38→Games contain 8, 16, or 32 units depending on complexity:
    39→
    40→| Units | Power | Typical Use |
    41→|-------|-------|-------------|
    42→| 8 (2³) | Standard | 8-bit games, simple 16-bit games |
    43→| 16 (2⁴) | Extended | Complex 16-bit games, early 32-bit |
    44→| 32 (2⁵) | Deep | Late 32-bit, 3D engines |
    45→
    46→Most 8-bit platform games use 8 units. The constraint forces focus - if a game concept needs more than 8 units on a simple platform, it's probably too ambitious for that point in the sequence.
    47→
    48→### Unit Duration and Outcome
    49→
    50→Each unit is:
    51→- **60-120 minutes** of focused work
    52→- **Self-contained** - produces a working, runnable result
    53→- **Incremental** - builds on the previous unit's code
    54→
    55→The learner never types for an hour only to see an error message. Every unit ends with something that works, even if incomplete.
    56→
    57→### Unit Naming
    58→
    59→**Full format:** `[Platform] Game N: [Name], Unit M`
    60→- Example: "C64 Game 1: SID Symphony, Unit 3"
    61→
    62→**Shorthand:** `[Platform]-[Game]-[Unit]`
    63→- Example: "C64-01-03"
    64→
    65→**Reference in Pattern Library/Vault:**
    66→- "Introduced in C64 Game 1: SID Symphony, Unit 3"
    67→- "See C64-01-03 for initial implementation"
    68→
    69→---
    70→
    71→## Games Per Platform
    72→
    73→Platforms vary enormously in complexity. A ZX81 curriculum shouldn't be the same size as a PlayStation curriculum. The number of games scales with platform depth:
    74→
    75→| Platform Size | Equivalent Lessons | Games | Examples |
    76→|---------------|-------------------|-------|----------|
    77→| Micro | ~512 | 2-4 | ZX81, VIC-20 |
    78→| Small | ~2,048 | 6-8 | Atari 2600, Game Boy |
    79→| Standard | ~4,096-8,192 | 12-16 | C64, ZX Spectrum, NES |
    80→| Large | ~8,192-16,384 | 16-24 | Amiga, Mega Drive |
    81→| Massive | ~16,384-32,768 | 24-48 | PlayStation, Saturn |
    82→
    83→"Equivalent lessons" is a rough guide for scoping, not a target. A platform has enough games when a learner completing them all could produce commercial-quality work for that platform.
    84→
    85→### Ordering Games
    86→
    87→Games are strictly ordered by complexity. Early games:
    88→- Use fewer hardware features
    89→- Have simpler game logic
    90→- Produce results quickly
    91→
    92→Later games:
    93→- Combine multiple techniques
    94→- Require deeper hardware understanding
    95→- Approach commercial quality
    96→
    97→A learner should be able to start at Game 1 with zero assembly knowledge and finish the final game with skills matching professional developers of that platform's era.
    98→
    99→---
   100→
   101→## Game 1: The Platform Hook
   102→
   103→Every platform's first game showcases its killer feature - the thing that made people buy that machine. This creates immediate emotional investment and demonstrates why this platform matters.
   104→
   105→| Platform | Killer Feature | Game 1 Concept |
   106→|----------|----------------|----------------|
   107→| Commodore 64 | SID sound chip | SID Symphony - rhythm game where you perform music |
   108→| ZX Spectrum | Ingenuity despite constraints | Platformer with beeper music |
   109→| NES | Tile-based PPU | Arena action built from tiles |
   110→| Amiga | Custom chips (Copper/Paula) | Audiovisual showcase |
   111→
   112→Game 1 should:
   113→- Produce impressive output early (Unit 1-2 should already feel special)
   114→- Be completable in 8 units
   115→- Teach fundamental platform concepts through the game's needs
   116→- Leave the learner wanting to build more
   117→
   118→Game 1 is not a tutorial. It's a statement: "This is what this machine can do. Now let's learn how."
   119→
   120→---
   121→
   122→## Pedagogical Approach
   123→
   124→The teaching style is modelled on 1980s British programming books: Usborne, INPUT magazine, the Home Computer Course. These materials taught through complete programs with rich visual annotation. The program was the lesson.
   125→
   126→### Program-First Teaching
   127→
   128→Every unit centres on a working program. Concepts are introduced when the program needs them, not in abstract isolation.
   129→
   130→**Not this:**
   131→> "Now we will learn about collision detection. Collision detection determines when two objects overlap..."
   132→
   133→**This:**
   134→> "The notes need to know when they've been hit. Here's how we detect the player pressing the right key at the right moment..."
   135→
   136→### Visual Density
   137→
   138→Units are visually rich:
   139→- Screenshots of expected output at each checkpoint
   140→- The finished result shown before the learner types a line
   141→- Diagrams where they clarify (memory maps, screen layouts)
   142→- Code surrounded by explanation, not buried in prose
   143→
   144→The learner should be able to flip through a unit and immediately understand what they're building.
   145→
   146→### Wonder-First Framing
   147→
   148→Lead with what's exciting, not what's technically accurate.
   149→
   150→**Not this:**
   151→> "This lesson covers the SID chip's ADSR envelope registers."
   152→
   153→**This:**
   154→> "Let's make the SID sing. By the end of this unit, pressing a key will play a note."
   155→
   156→Technical accuracy matters, but it serves the wonder, not the other way around.
   157→
   158→### Single-Session Completion
   159→
   160→Each unit produces a working result in one sitting. The learner should never:
   161→- Stop mid-unit with broken code
   162→- Wait until "next time" to see something run
   163→- Type for an hour only to see an error
   164→
   165→If a unit can't be completed in 60-120 minutes, it's too big. Split it.
   166→
   167→---
   168→
   169→## Voice and Tone
   170→
   171→### The Magazine Voice
   172→
   173→The framing borrows from 1980s British gaming magazines - Your Sinclair, ZZAP!64, Crash. Irreverent, enthusiastic, occasionally conspiratorial. The reader is a co-conspirator, not a student.
   174→
   175→**Not this:**
   176→> "In this unit, you will learn to configure the VIC-II sprite registers."
   177→
   178→**This:**
   179→> "Don't panic. The BASIC stub is just a magic incantation that launches our machine code. Type it exactly, and we'll explain what it's doing once we're safely in assembly land."
   180→
   181→The instruction itself remains precise and thorough. The irreverence is in the framing, not the technical content.
   182→
   183→### Honesty About Difficulty
   184→
   185→Some things are hard. Say so.
   186→
   187→> "Calculating row × 40 in 6502 is a faff. There's no multiply instruction, so we'll use a lookup table. It's not elegant, but it's fast."
   188→
   189→Don't pretend everything is easy. Don't apologise for difficulty either. Just acknowledge it and move on.
   190→
   191→### British English
   192→
   193→Use British spelling throughout:
   194→- colour, grey, centre, organise, learnt
   195→
   196→**Exception:** "program" for computer programs (not "programme").
   197→
   198→### What to Avoid
   199→
   200→- Marketing speak ("amazing", "incredible", "revolutionary")
   201→- Excessive exclamation marks
   202→- Condescension ("As you can see...", "Simply...", "Just...")
   203→- Apologies for the platform ("Unfortunately, the C64 can only...")
   204→- Forced nostalgia ("Back in the day...", "Remember when...")
   205→
   206→---
   207→
   208→## BASIC Gateway (Optional)
   209→
   210→Some platforms have an accessible high-level language - BASIC, AMOS, Forth. For these platforms, an optional BASIC track provides a gentler entry point before assembly.
   211→
   212→### When to Include a BASIC Gateway
   213→
   214→Include if the platform has:
   215→- A built-in or widely-available high-level language
   216→- Historical use for hobbyist/learning development
   217→- The ability to produce immediate visual/interactive results
   218→
   219→| Platform | Gateway Language | Include? |
   220→|----------|------------------|----------|
   221→| Commodore 64 | BASIC V2 | Yes |
   222→| ZX Spectrum | Sinclair BASIC | Yes |
   223→| Amiga | AMOS BASIC | Yes |
   224→| NES | None | No - assembly only |
   225→
   226→### Scope
   227→
   228→The BASIC track is:
   229→- **1-2 games maximum** - enough to learn fundamentals, not a complete curriculum
   230→- **Separate from the main sequence** - Game 1 in assembly is still Game 1
   231→- **Optional** - learners can skip directly to assembly if preferred
   232→
   233→BASIC games should demonstrate both the language's capabilities and its limitations. By the end, learners should understand why assembly exists without being told BASIC is bad.
   234→
   235→### Not a Prerequisite
   236→
   237→The assembly curriculum assumes no BASIC knowledge. A learner starting at Game 1 (assembly) should not feel they've missed something by skipping BASIC.
   238→
   239→---
   240→
   241→## Standalone Technique Examples
   242→
   243→Some techniques benefit from isolated demonstration before integration into a game. The `techniques/` directory provides minimal, focused examples.
   244→
   245→### The Three-Level Model
   246→
   247→```
   248→Unit (game context)          → "Why do I need this? How does it fit?"
   249→Standalone (isolated demo)   → "How does this technique work on its own?"
   250→Pattern Library (reference)  → "Give me production-ready code to copy"
   251→```
   252→
   253→Each level serves a different purpose:
   254→- **Units** teach techniques within game context (game-first pedagogy)
   255→- **Standalone demos** provide isolated reference (50-100 lines, just the technique)
   256→- **Patterns** provide optimised, production-ready implementations
   257→
   258→### Directory Structure
   259→
   260→```
   261→code-samples/{platform}/{game}/
   262→  techniques/                    # Standalone demos introduced by this game
   263→    sprite-multiplexing.asm      # Minimal isolated demonstration
   264→    raster-interrupts.asm
   265→  unit-01/
   266→  unit-12/                       # Unit that teaches the technique in context
   267→    game.asm                     # Full game integration
   268→```
   269→
   270→### When to Create Standalone Demos
   271→
   272→Create a standalone demo when a technique:
   273→- Is complex enough that seeing it in isolation aids understanding
   274→- Will be reused across multiple games
   275→- Is a likely Pattern Library candidate
   276→- Benefits from experimentation outside game context
   277→
   278→**Not everything needs a standalone demo.** Simple techniques (reading a joystick, playing a sound effect) are better taught purely in game context.
   279→
   280→### Standalone Demo Requirements
   281→
   282→A standalone demo should:
   283→- Be **minimal** - 50-100 lines, absolute maximum 150
   284→- Be **complete** - compile and run without dependencies
   285→- Be **focused** - demonstrate exactly one technique
   286→- **Include comments** - enough to understand without the unit
   287→- **Produce visible output** - show that it works
   288→
   289→Example header:
   290→```asm
   291→; =============================================================================
   292→; SPRITE MULTIPLEXING - STANDALONE DEMO
   293→; Demonstrates displaying >8 sprites using raster interrupts
   294→; Full tutorial: Game 9 (Sprite Storm), Unit 12
   295→; =============================================================================
   296→```
   297→
   298→### Relationship to Units
   299→
   300→Units link to standalone demos:
   301→```markdown
   302→> **Isolated Example:** Want to study multiplexing without the game code?
   303→> See [techniques/sprite-multiplexing.asm](link) for a minimal demonstration.
   304→```
   305→
   306→The unit teaches *why* and *when*; the standalone shows *how* in isolation.
   307→
   308→---
   309→
   310→## Integration with Vault and Pattern Library
   311→
   312→Units don't exist in isolation. They link to the Vault for context and the Pattern Library for reusable code.
   313→
   314→### The Vault
   315→
   316→The Vault is an encyclopedia of people, companies, games, techniques, hardware, events, and culture. Units link to Vault entries when context enriches understanding.
   317→
   318→**Inline links:**
   319→> "This sprite multiplexing technique was pioneered by [Andrew Braybrook](/vault/people/andrew-braybrook) in [Uridium](/vault/games/uridium)."
   320→
   321→**When to link:**
   322→- The person/game/technique is directly relevant to what's being built
   323→- The link adds context the learner might want to explore
   324→- The reference is specific, not generic
   325→
   326→**When not to link:**
   327→- Every unit mentioning sprites doesn't need a VIC-II link
   328→- Forced connections ("This was released the same year as...")
   329→- Links that interrupt the flow of instruction
   330→
   331→### The Pattern Library
   332→
   333→The Pattern Library contains production-ready code patterns. Units teach concepts; patterns provide copy-paste implementations.
   334→
   335→**Reference format:**
   336→> "For a complete, optimised implementation, see [Sprite Multiplexing (Basic)](/patterns/c64/rendering/sprite-multiplexing-basic)."
   337→
   338→**Pattern evolution:**
   339→Patterns exist at multiple complexity levels. Reference the appropriate version:
   340→- "C64-01-03 introduces the basic pattern"
   341→- "C64-08-05 covers the optimised version"
   342→
   343→Units introduce techniques. Patterns provide the reference implementation. The Vault provides the history.
   344→
   345→---
   346→
   347→## Platform Curriculum Files
   348→
   349→This specification defines how curricula work. The actual game sequences live in platform-specific files:
   350→
   351→- `/docs/curriculum/commodore-64-curriculum.md`
   352→- `/docs/curriculum/sinclair-zx-spectrum-curriculum.md`
   353→- `/docs/curriculum/commodore-amiga-curriculum.md`
   354→- `/docs/curriculum/nintendo-nes-curriculum.md`
   355→
   356→Each platform curriculum file contains:
   357→
   358→1. **Platform overview** - The platform's killer feature and teaching focus
   359→2. **Game sequence** - Numbered list of games with brief descriptions
   360→3. **Game details** - For each game: name, unit count, concept, skills taught
   361→4. **BASIC gateway** (if applicable) - Separate section for BASIC track games
   362→
   363→Platform curriculum files should be concise. They list what exists, not why the model works - that's this document's job.
   364→
   365→---
   366→
   367→## Version History
   368→
   369→- **2.1 (2026-01-19):** Added Standalone Technique Examples section (three-level model: units → standalone demos → patterns).
   370→- **2.0 (2025-11-25):** Complete rewrite. Replaced phases/tiers/lessons model with games model.
   371→- **1.0 (2025-01-15):** Original specification using 4,096 lessons per platform.
   372→

     2→title: "Shadowkeep"
     3→description: "A top-down maze explorer where the Spectrum's attribute system IS the game — ink and paper define walls, floors, items, and danger."
     4→game: 1
     5→gameName: "Shadowkeep"
     6→totalUnits: 128
     7→system: "Sinclair ZX Spectrum"
     8→layout: "../../../../layouts/GameLayout.astro"
     9→status: "in-progress"
    10→units: []
    11→---
    12→
    13→## Coming Soon
    14→
    15→Shadowkeep is currently in development. Check back soon!
    16→
    17→---
    18→
    19→## What You'll Build
    20→
    21→Top-down maze explorer. Navigate rooms of a dark keep, collect keys, avoid creatures, find the exit.
    22→
    23→The attribute system is the game. Walls are INK colour, floors are PAPER, items are BRIGHT, hazards FLASH. Collision detection is a single attribute read. The Spectrum's most famous limitation — colour clash — becomes the core mechanic.
    24→
    25→## Why This Game?
    26→
    27→The ZX Spectrum's attribute system is what made Spectrum games look distinctly *Spectrum*. Each 8×8 pixel block shares one foreground colour and one background colour. Most games fight this constraint. Shadowkeep embraces it.
    28→
    29→Every room is built from attributes. You'll learn:
    30→
    31→- **Screen memory layout** — the infamous non-linear thirds
    32→- **Attribute memory** — INK, PAPER, BRIGHT, FLASH bits
    33→- **Wall collision** — reading attributes to detect walls
    34→- **Room data structures** — tile maps for multi-room worlds
    35→- **Item collection** — keys, doors, and inventory
    36→- **Enemy patrol AI** — simple path-following creatures
    37→- **Beeper sound effects** — pitch variation for audio feedback
    38→
    39→By the end, you'll have a complete maze explorer worthy of the Spectrum's golden age.
    40→
    41→## Skills You'll Master
    42→
    43→- **Attribute system** — INK, PAPER, BRIGHT, FLASH
    44→- **Screen memory** — non-linear layout and thirds
    45→- **Character-cell movement** — 8-pixel grid navigation
    46→- **Wall collision** — attribute-based detection
    47→- **Room data** — tile maps and transitions
    48→- **Item collection** — keys, doors, inventory
    49→- **Enemy AI** — patrol paths and chase behaviour
    50→- **Beeper sound** — pitch variation effects
    51→- **Multi-room worlds** — room transitions and state
    52→
    53→## Prerequisites
    54→
    55→This is Game 1. No prerequisites — just enthusiasm and a willingness to learn Z80 assembly.
    56→
    57→## Time Investment
    58→
    59→128 units at 60-90 minutes each. Roughly 128-192 hours total.
    60→

1→---
     2→title: "A Coloured Block"
     3→description: "Write to attribute memory to place coloured blocks on the ZX Spectrum screen — the first step toward building a maze."
     4→pubDate: 2026-02-06
     5→game: 1
     6→gameName: "Shadowkeep"
     7→unit: 1
     8→totalUnits: 128
     9→system: "Sinclair ZX Spectrum"
    10→tags: ["assembly", "z80", "attributes", "memory"]
    11→nextLesson: "/sinclair-zx-spectrum/assembly/game-01-shadowkeep/unit-02"
    12→layout: "../../../../layouts/UnitLayout.astro"
    13→---
    14→
    15→import CodeFromFile from "@components/CodeFromFile.astro";
    16→
    17→Black screen. No sound, no movement. Then a block of colour appears — a wall in the darkness.
    18→
    19→This is how the ZX Spectrum works. Every colour on screen comes from *attribute memory* — a grid of 768 bytes starting at address $5800. Write a value there and colour appears. The wall is just a byte. The floor is just a byte. By the end of this unit, you'll place both on screen — the first stones of Shadowkeep's maze.
    20→
    21→## The Empty Program
    22→
    23→The simplest Z80 program:
    24→
    25→```z80
    26→            org     32768
    27→start:
    28→            ret
    29→            end     start
    30→```
    31→
    32→`org 32768` places the code at address 32768 ($8000) — a safe area above BASIC's workspace. `ret` returns to BASIC immediately. `end start` tells the assembler where execution begins.
    33→
    34→Assemble it:
    35→
    36→```bash
    37→pasmonext --tapbas shadowkeep.asm shadowkeep.tap
    38→```
    39→
    40→Load the TAP file in Fuse (or your emulator). Type `RUN` — nothing happens. The program runs and returns to BASIC instantly. That's correct. Your toolchain works.
    41→
    42→## Set the Border
    43→
    44→Two instructions change the border colour:
    45→
    46→<CodeFromFile src="sinclair-zx-spectrum/game-01-shadowkeep/unit-01/snippets/01-border.asm" />
    47→
    48→`ld a, 1` loads the value 1 into the **A register** (the accumulator). In Z80 assembly, bare numbers are always immediate values — no special prefix needed.
    49→
    50→`out ($fe), a` sends that value to **port $FE**, which controls the border colour. The Spectrum uses ports to communicate with hardware — `out` writes to a port, `in` reads from one.
    51→
    52→The `halt` / `jr .loop` pair keeps the program running forever. Without it, execution returns to BASIC and the border resets. `halt` waits for the next video frame (1/50th of a second), and `jr .loop` jumps back.
    53→
    54→Assemble and run. The border turns blue.
    55→
    56→| Value | Colour  |
    57→|-------|---------|
    58→| 0     | Black   |
    59→| 1     | Blue    |
    60→| 2     | Red     |
    61→| 3     | Magenta |
    62→| 4     | Green   |
    63→| 5     | Cyan    |
    64→| 6     | Yellow  |
    65→| 7     | White   |
    66→
    67→Try changing the `1` to other values. Each gives a different border colour.
    68→
    69→## One Coloured Cell
    70→
    71→Now for the real thing — writing directly to screen memory:
    72→
    73→<CodeFromFile src="sinclair-zx-spectrum/game-01-shadowkeep/unit-01/snippets/02-one-cell.asm" />
    74→
    75→The screen clear at the top uses `ldir` — an instruction we haven't learnt yet. It fills screen memory with zeros, giving us a clean black canvas. We'll explore how it works in Unit 3. For now, trust that it clears the screen.
    76→
    77→The important lines are:
    78→
    79→```z80
    80→            ld      a, $30
    81→            ld      ($5990), a
    82→```
    83→
    84→`ld a, $30` loads the hex value $30 (48 decimal) into A. `ld ($5990), a` stores A at memory address $5990. That address sits inside *attribute memory* — and the Spectrum immediately shows a yellow block on screen.
    85→
    86→No function call. No graphics API. Just a byte written to the right address.
    87→
    88→### Why $5990?
    89→
    90→Attribute memory starts at $5800 and contains 768 bytes — one per character cell in a 32-column, 24-row grid. Each byte controls the colours of an 8×8 pixel area.
    91→
    92→To find the address for any cell:
    93→
    94→```
    95→Address = $5800 + (row × 32) + column
    96→```
    97→
    98→Our yellow block sits at row 12, column 16:
    99→
   100→```
   101→$5800 + (12 × 32) + 16 = $5800 + 384 + 16 = $5990
   102→```
   103→
   104→Rows run 0–23 (top to bottom). Columns run 0–31 (left to right).
   105→
   106→## The Attribute Byte
   107→
   108→The value $30 encodes both colours for the cell. Here's the format:
   109→
   110→```
   111→Bit:  7      6       5  4  3     2  1  0
   112→      FLASH  BRIGHT  P  P  P     I  I  I
   113→                     PAPER        INK
   114→```
   115→
   116→- **FLASH** (bit 7) — alternates INK and PAPER colours when set
   117→- **BRIGHT** (bit 6) — brighter versions of the colours when set
   118→- **PAPER** (bits 5–3) — background colour (0–7, same table as border)
   119→- **INK** (bits 2–0) — foreground colour (0–7)
   120→
   121→$30 in binary is `00110000`:
   122→
   123→```
   124→0  0  1  1  0  0  0  0
   125→F  B  ---P---  ---I---
   126→      1  1  0  0  0  0
   127→         6        0
   128→```
   129→
   130→PAPER = 110 = 6 = yellow. INK = 000 = 0 = black. No FLASH, no BRIGHT.
   131→
   132→The cell appears as a solid yellow block because the bitmap beneath is all zeros — so only the PAPER colour shows.
   133→
   134→## Walls and Floor
   135→
   136→One cell proves the concept. Multiple cells start to look like a room:
   137→
   138→<CodeFromFile src="sinclair-zx-spectrum/game-01-shadowkeep/unit-01/snippets/03-walls-and-floor.asm" />
   139→
   140→Wall cells use $09 — PAPER 1 (blue) + INK 1 (blue) — a solid blue block. Floor cells use $38 — PAPER 7 (white) + INK 0 (black) — a white block.
   141→
   142→Load A with the wall colour once, then store it to multiple wall addresses. Change A to the floor colour, store to floor addresses. Same two instructions — `ld a` and `ld (addr), a` — just different addresses and values.
   143→
   144→It's tedious writing every cell by hand. That's deliberate — in Unit 3, you'll learn a loop instruction that fills an entire row in three lines. But first, understand what you're automating.
   145→
   146→## Try This: Change the Wall Colour
   147→
   148→In the complete program below, find:
   149→
   150→```z80
   151→WALL        equ     $09             ; PAPER 1 (blue) + INK 1
   152→```
   153→
   154→Change `$09` to `$11` for red walls (PAPER 2), or `$21` for green walls (PAPER 4). Work out the value: shift the colour number left by 3 bits, then add the same number as INK for a solid block.
   155→
   156→| PAPER | Colour  | Solid value |
   157→|-------|---------|-------------|
   158→| 0     | Black   | $00         |
   159→| 1     | Blue    | $09         |
   160→| 2     | Red     | $12         |
   161→| 3     | Magenta | $1b         |
   162→| 4     | Green   | $24         |
   163→| 5     | Cyan    | $2d         |
   164→| 6     | Yellow  | $36         |
   165→| 7     | White   | $3f         |
   166→
   167→## Try This: BRIGHT and FLASH
   168→
   169→Find the treasure cell in the complete program:
   170→
   171→```z80
   172→TREASURE    equ     $70             ; BRIGHT + PAPER 6 (yellow)
   173→```
   174→
   175→That $70 has bit 6 set — BRIGHT. The yellow is noticeably brighter than a regular yellow cell would be. Try changing it to $30 (no BRIGHT) and compare.
   176→
   177→Now look at the hazard:
   178→
   179→```z80
   180→HAZARD      equ     $90             ; FLASH + PAPER 2 (red)
   181→```
   182→
   183→$90 has bit 7 set — FLASH. The cell alternates between its normal colours and their inverse, creating a flashing effect. This will mark danger in Shadowkeep.
   184→
   185→Try combining both: $D0 would be FLASH + BRIGHT + PAPER 2. Bright flashing red.
   186→
   187→## Try This: Calculate an Address
   188→
   189→Where would a cell appear at row 5, column 20?
   190→
   191→```
   192→$5800 + (5 × 32) + 20 = $5800 + 160 + 20 = $5800 + 180 = $58B4
   193→```
   194→
   195→Add these lines before the `.loop` in the complete program:
   196→
   197→```z80
   198→            ld      a, $2d          ; Cyan (PAPER 5 + INK 5)
   199→            ld      ($58b4), a
   200→```
   201→
   202→Reassemble and run. A cyan block appears at row 5, column 20 — separate from the room.
   203→
   204→## The Complete Code
   205→
   206→<CodeFromFile src="sinclair-zx-spectrum/game-01-shadowkeep/unit-01/shadowkeep.asm" />
   207→
   208→![Shadowkeep Unit 1](/images/sinclair-zx-spectrum/game-01-shadowkeep/unit-01/screenshot.png)
   209→
   210→A room takes shape. Blue walls surround white floor. A bright yellow cell marks treasure. A flashing red cell marks danger. All from attribute writes — no graphics routines, no sprites, just bytes at the right addresses.
   211→
   212→## If It Doesn't Work
   213→
   214→- **No colour?** Check you're writing to $5800+ (attributes), not $4000+ (bitmap). The attribute range is $5800–$5AFF.
   215→- **Wrong position?** Address = $5800 + (row × 32) + column. Rows 0–23, columns 0–31. An address off by 32 means wrong row; off by 1 means wrong column.
   216→- **Screen not black?** The `ldir` clear must cover from $4000. If you only clear attributes ($5800), leftover BASIC text may show through coloured cells.
   217→- **Assembler error?** Check `org 32768` is at the top and `end start` is at the bottom. The label `start:` must match.
   218→- **Program returns to BASIC?** You need the `halt` / `jr .loop` pair at the end to keep running.
   219→
   220→## What You've Learnt
   221→
   222→- **LD A, value** — load an immediate value into the accumulator. This is how you prepare data for the hardware.
   223→- **LD (address), A** — store the accumulator to a memory address. This is how you write to the screen.
   224→- **OUT (port), A** — write to a hardware port. Port $FE controls the border colour.
   225→- **Attribute memory** — $5800 to $5AFF, 768 bytes, one per 8×8 cell. Address = $5800 + (row × 32) + column.
   226→- **The attribute byte** — FBPPPIII: flash, bright, paper colour, ink colour. One byte controls everything about a cell's colour.
   227→- **Attributes as game world** — blue means wall, white means floor, bright means treasure, flash means danger. Colour IS gameplay on the Spectrum.
   228→
   229→## What's Next
   230→
   231→You placed colours on screen by hand — one address per cell. In Unit 2, you'll pull apart the attribute byte with `AND` and `OR`, learning to read and change individual bits. That's how the game will check what's at any position: read the attribute, check the colour, and know whether it's wall, floor, or something else entirely.
   232→

1→---
     2→title: "Reading Colour"
     3→description: "Use AND and OR to extract and modify individual bits of the attribute byte — the foundation of collision detection."
     4→pubDate: 2026-02-06
     5→game: 1
     6→gameName: "Shadowkeep"
     7→unit: 2
     8→totalUnits: 128
     9→system: "Sinclair ZX Spectrum"
    10→tags: ["assembly", "z80", "attributes", "and-or"]
    11→prevLesson: "/sinclair-zx-spectrum/assembly/game-01-shadowkeep/unit-01"
    12→nextLesson: "/sinclair-zx-spectrum/assembly/game-01-shadowkeep/unit-03"
    13→layout: "../../../../layouts/UnitLayout.astro"
    14→---
    15→
    16→import CodeFromFile from "@components/CodeFromFile.astro";
    17→
    18→The room from Unit 1 is drawn — blue walls, white floor, yellow treasure, red hazard. But how does the game *know* what's at each cell?
    19→
    20→It reads the attribute byte. `AND` extracts the bits you care about. `OR` sets the bits you want to change. These two instructions are how Shadowkeep will detect walls, collect treasure, and trigger hazards. Every interaction in the game reduces to reading an attribute and checking its bits.
    21→
    22→## Reading an Attribute
    23→
    24→In Unit 1 you wrote attributes with `ld (address), a`. Reading works in reverse:
    25→
    26→```z80
    27→            ld      a, ($596f)      ; Read attribute at row 11, col 15
    28→```
    29→
    30→After this instruction, A holds the attribute byte at that address. For a floor cell, that's $38 (white PAPER, black INK). For a wall, it's $09 (blue). The value is whatever you — or the game — last wrote there.
    31→
    32→This is the basis of everything. Want to know if a cell is a wall? Read it. Want to check for treasure? Read it. The screen itself is the game state.
    33→
    34→## AND — Extracting Bits
    35→
    36→The attribute byte packs four pieces of information into eight bits:
    37→
    38→```
    39→Bit:  7      6       5  4  3     2  1  0
    40→      FLASH  BRIGHT  P  P  P     I  I  I
    41→                     PAPER        INK
    42→```
    43→
    44→`AND` keeps only the bits you specify and zeros everything else. The value you AND with is called a *mask*.
    45→
    46→<CodeFromFile src="sinclair-zx-spectrum/game-01-shadowkeep/unit-02/snippets/01-read-and-check.asm" />
    47→
    48→`AND $07` is the mask `00000111` — it keeps bits 0, 1, and 2 (the INK colour) and zeros bits 3–7. If the cell was $09 (PAPER 1, INK 1), after `AND $07` A holds just 1 — the INK colour.
    49→
    50→`CP 1` compares A with 1. If they're equal, the Z80 sets the **Zero flag**, and `JR Z` (jump if zero) takes the branch. INK 1 means blue, which means wall. That's collision detection in three instructions.
    51→
    52→### Common Masks
    53→
    54→| Mask | Binary | Extracts |
    55→|------|--------|----------|
    56→| `$07` | `00000111` | INK colour (0–7) |
    57→| `$38` | `00111000` | PAPER bits (still shifted — value is 0, 8, 16... not 0–7) |
    58→| `$40` | `01000000` | BRIGHT bit (result is 0 or 64) |
    59→| `$80` | `10000000` | FLASH bit (result is 0 or 128) |
    60→
    61→To check BRIGHT: `AND $40` — if the result isn't zero, BRIGHT is set. To check FLASH: `AND $80`.
    62→
    63→To check PAPER colour, you'd need to shift the result right by 3 bits. We'll learn shift instructions later. For now, compare against the shifted value directly: `AND $38` then `CP $08` checks for PAPER 1 (blue).
    64→
    65→## OR — Setting Bits
    66→
    67→`OR` sets bits to 1 without disturbing the others. It's the opposite of AND: where AND clears, OR adds.
    68→
    69→<CodeFromFile src="sinclair-zx-spectrum/game-01-shadowkeep/unit-02/snippets/02-set-bright.asm" />
    70→
    71→The floor cell was $38 (`00111000`). `OR $40` is `01000000` — it sets bit 6 without touching any other bit:
    72→
    73→```
    74→  00111000   ($38 — white floor)
    75→OR 01000000   ($40 — BRIGHT mask)
    76→= 01111000   ($78 — bright white floor)
    77→```
    78→
    79→The cell becomes visibly brighter. One instruction changed one property without affecting the colour.
    80→
    81→## AND — Clearing Bits
    82→
    83→AND can also *clear* bits. Use the inverse of the bit you want to remove:
    84→
    85→```z80
    86→            ld      a, ($59b1)      ; Read hazard cell
    87→            and     $7f             ; Clear FLASH (bit 7)
    88→            ld      ($59b1), a      ; Write back — no longer flashing
    89→```
    90→
    91→`$7F` is `01111111` — every bit is 1 except bit 7. AND with this keeps everything *except* FLASH:
    92→
    93→```
    94→  10010000   ($90 — FLASH + red)
    95→AND 01111111   ($7F — all except bit 7)
    96→= 00010000   ($10 — just red, no flash)
    97→```
    98→
    99→The hazard stops flashing. It's been defused.
   100→
   101→### Clearing Masks
   102→
   103→| Mask | Binary | Clears |
   104→|------|--------|--------|
   105→| `$7F` | `01111111` | FLASH |
   106→| `$BF` | `10111111` | BRIGHT |
   107→| `$C7` | `11000111` | PAPER |
   108→| `$F8` | `11111000` | INK |
   109→| `$C0` | `11000000` | PAPER + INK (keeps only FLASH and BRIGHT) |
   110→
   111→Each mask is the bitwise inverse of the extraction mask. To clear BRIGHT ($40), AND with $BF (NOT $40).
   112→
   113→## AND + OR — Changing Colours
   114→
   115→To change a colour, clear the old bits then set the new ones:
   116→
   117→<CodeFromFile src="sinclair-zx-spectrum/game-01-shadowkeep/unit-02/snippets/03-change-paper.asm" />
   118→
   119→Two steps: AND strips the colour away, OR paints the new one on. This pattern — **read, mask, set, write** — is the fundamental operation for every attribute modification in the game.
   120→
   121→## Try This: Brighten a Wall
   122→
   123→The wall cells are $09 (blue, no BRIGHT). Add BRIGHT:
   124→
   125→```z80
   126→            ld      a, ($594e)      ; Read wall (row 10, col 14)
   127→            or      $40             ; Set BRIGHT
   128→            ld      ($594e), a      ; Bright blue wall
   129→```
   130→
   131→Add this before the `.loop` in the complete program. The wall cell becomes noticeably brighter blue. Compare it to the neighbouring walls that are still regular blue.
   132→
   133→## Try This: Check a Cell
   134→
   135→This pattern checks what type of cell is at an address:
   136→
   137→```z80
   138→            ld      a, ($5990)      ; Read cell
   139→            and     $07             ; Extract INK
   140→            cp      0               ; INK 0 = floor/treasure
   141→            jr      z, not_wall
   142→            cp      1               ; INK 1 = wall
   143→            jr      z, wall_found
   144→```
   145→
   146→This is exactly how Unit 7 will implement wall collision. The game reads the attribute at the player's target position and decides whether movement is allowed.
   147→
   148→## Try This: Swap Colours
   149→
   150→Change the floor from white to cyan. You need to clear PAPER (AND $C7), then set PAPER 5 (OR $28):
   151→
   152→```z80
   153→            ld      a, ($596f)      ; Read floor cell
   154→            and     $c7             ; Clear PAPER bits
   155→            or      $28             ; Set PAPER 5 (cyan)
   156→            ld      ($596f), a
   157→```
   158→
   159→$28 = `00101000`. Bits 5–3 = `101` = 5 = cyan.
   160→
   161→## The Complete Code
   162→
   163→The program draws the Unit 1 room, then modifies cells to demonstrate each operation:
   164→
   165→- **OR $40** brightens three floor cells (row 11)
   166→- **OR $80** makes the treasure flash (it was only BRIGHT before)
   167→- **AND $7F** defuses the hazard (removes FLASH, leaving static red)
   168→- **AND $C0 + OR $12** changes one wall from blue to red
   169→
   170→<CodeFromFile src="sinclair-zx-spectrum/game-01-shadowkeep/unit-02/shadowkeep.asm" />
   171→
   172→![Shadowkeep Unit 2](/images/sinclair-zx-spectrum/game-01-shadowkeep/unit-02/screenshot.png)
   173→
   174→Compare this with Unit 1. The top floor row is brighter. One wall cell in the top row has turned red. The treasure now flashes. The hazard is static. All done by reading attributes and changing their bits.
   175→
   176→## If It Doesn't Work
   177→
   178→- **OR didn't change anything?** Make sure you write the result back with `ld (addr), a`. Without the store, the change stays in A and the screen doesn't update.
   179→- **AND gave zero?** That means the bits you tested aren't set. A floor cell has INK 0, so `AND $07` gives 0 — that's correct, not an error.
   180→- **Wrong cell changed?** Double-check the address. Off by 1 = wrong column. Off by 32 = wrong row.
   181→- **Colour looks wrong?** Remember PAPER bits are shifted left by 3. PAPER 2 (red) is $10, not $02. Use the table above.
   182→
   183→## What You've Learnt
   184→
   185→- **LD A,(address)** — read an attribute byte from screen memory. The screen is readable, not just writable.
   186→- **AND mask** — extract specific bits. AND $07 gives the INK colour. AND $40 checks BRIGHT. AND $80 checks FLASH.
   187→- **OR mask** — set specific bits. OR $40 adds BRIGHT. OR $80 adds FLASH. Other bits are untouched.
   188→- **AND to clear** — AND with the inverse mask removes bits. AND $7F clears FLASH. AND $BF clears BRIGHT.
   189→- **Read-modify-write** — the pattern for changing any attribute: read, AND to clear, OR to set, write back.
   190→- **CP and JR Z** — compare A with a value and branch if equal. This is how the game will detect walls: read INK, compare, decide.
   191→
   192→## What's Next
   193→
   194→Writing cells by hand is tedious — 25 cells meant 25 separate writes. In Unit 3, you'll learn `DJNZ`, a loop instruction that repeats a block of code. One loop will draw an entire row of walls. Another will fill the floor. The room will be built in a fraction of the code.
   195→

1→---
     2→title: "Shatter"
     3→description: "A Breakout clone teaching custom fonts, smooth pixel movement, and beeper sound design."
     4→game: 2
     5→gameName: "Shatter"
     6→totalUnits: 128
     7→system: "ZX Spectrum"
     8→layout: "../../../../layouts/GameLayout.astro"
     9→status: "coming-soon"
    10→units: []
    11→---
    12→
    13→## Coming Soon
    14→
    15→Shatter is currently in development. Check back soon!
    16→
    17→---
    18→
    19→## What You'll Build
    20→
    21→Breakout clone with polish focus. Paddle, ball, bricks — with custom font, smooth animation, and audio feedback.
    22→
    23→Control a paddle at the bottom of the screen. Bounce the ball to destroy bricks above. Simple mechanics, but this game teaches you how to achieve smooth movement on the Spectrum — something many games got wrong.
    24→
    25→## Why This Game?
    26→
    27→After Ink War taught you the attribute system, Shatter focuses on **smooth movement** and **custom graphics**. The Spectrum doesn't have sprites, so everything is software-rendered. You'll learn:
    28→
    29→- Design and install **custom fonts**
    30→- Achieve **sub-cell movement** (pixel precision, not character precision)
    31→- Implement **frame-based timing** for consistent speed
    32→- Build proper **collision detection** (walls, paddle, bricks)
    33→- Calculate **bounce angles** and reflection physics
    34→- Create **beeper sound effects** that don't slow the game
    35→
    36→By the end, you'll have a polished Breakout that feels professional.
    37→
    38→## Skills You'll Master
    39→
    40→- **Custom font design** — creating and installing character sets
    41→- **Smooth movement** — pixel-precision animation
    42→- **Frame timing** — delta movement for consistent speed
    43→- **Collision detection** — walls, paddle, bricks
    44→- **Bounce physics** — angles and reflection
    45→- **Visual effects** — brick destruction feedback
    46→- **Beeper sound** — audio without slowdown
    47→- **Power-ups** — game modifiers
    48→- **Level progression** — increasing difficulty
    49→
    50→## Prerequisites
    51→
    52→Complete **Game 1: Shadowkeep** first. You'll need the screen memory and keyboard skills from that game.
    53→
    54→## Time Investment
    55→
    56→64 units at 60-90 minutes each. Roughly 64-96 hours total, spread across 4 phases.
    57→

1→# Sinclair ZX Spectrum Curriculum
     2→
     3→**Platform:** Sinclair ZX Spectrum
     4→**Killer Feature:** Attribute colour system
     5→**Games:** 16
     6→**Total Units:** 3,584
     7→
     8→---
     9→
    10→## Overview
    11→
    12→The ZX Spectrum curriculum teaches Z80 assembly programming through 16 complete games. The sequence begins with the attribute system - the Spectrum's defining characteristic that created its distinctive visual style - and progresses through scrolling, enemy AI, isometric projection, and advanced techniques until learners can produce commercial-quality games.
    13→
    14→The Spectrum had no hardware sprites, no smooth scrolling, and a single-bit beeper for sound. Every impressive effect was achieved through software and creativity. This curriculum embraces those constraints, teaching learners to make the hardware sing through ingenuity.
    15→
    16→By the final game, learners will have skills matching professional Spectrum developers of the late 1980s - equivalent to teams at Ultimate, Ocean, or Codemasters.
    17→
    18→---
    19→
    20→## Unit Structure
    21→
    22→Games scale in unit count based on complexity:
    23→
    24→| Games | Units Each | Total | Rationale |
    25→|-------|-----------|-------|-----------|
    26→| 1-8 | 128 | 1,024 | Foundation games - maze explorer, physics, platformer, exploration, strategy, maze, snake, Tetris |
    27→| 9-10 | 256 | 512 | Intermediate - scrolling, character-based display mastery |
    28→| 11-13 | 256 | 768 | System-heavy - multiple complex systems |
    29→| 14 | 256 | 256 | Advanced - Filmation-style isometric (Head Over Heels) |
    30→| 15 | 512 | 512 | Expert - Knight Lore-style isometric with transformation |
    31→| 16 | 512 | 512 | Capstone - commercial quality with 128K/48K dual support |
    32→| **Total** | | **3,584** | |
    33→
    34→Each game follows a phase structure (4-16 phases depending on length), with each phase containing 16 units.
    35→
    36→---
    37→
    38→## Game Sequence
    39→
    40→### Game 1: Shadowkeep
    41→**Units:** 128 (8 phases)
    42→**Concept:** Top-down maze explorer. Navigate rooms of a dark keep, collect keys, avoid creatures, find the exit. The attribute system is the game — ink and paper define walls, floors, items, and danger.
    43→
    44→**Skills taught:**
    45→- Screen memory layout ($4000-$57FF)
    46→- Attribute memory ($5800-$5AFF)
    47→- INK, PAPER, BRIGHT, FLASH bits
    48→- Keyboard input via ROM routines
    49→- Character-cell movement (8-pixel grid)
    50→- Wall collision via attribute reading
    51→- Room data structures (tile maps)
    52→- Item collection and inventory
    53→- Enemy patrol AI (simple path following)
    54→- Beeper sound effects (pitch variation)
    55→- Multi-room transitions
    56→- Key and door mechanics
    57→- Score and status display
    58→- Increasing difficulty (more rooms, faster enemies)
    59→
    60→**Why first:** A maze explorer makes the attribute system the core mechanic from Unit 1. Walls are INK, floors are PAPER, items are BRIGHT — the Spectrum's defining feature becomes the game design tool. Learners see pixels on screen immediately and navigate a room by Unit 3.
    61→
    62→**Detailed outline:** `/docs/curriculum/assembly/sinclair-zx-spectrum/game-01-full-game-outline-zx-spectrum-shadowkeep.md`
    63→
    64→---
    65→
    66→### Game 2: Shatter
    67→**Units:** 128 (8 phases)
    68→**Concept:** Breakout clone polished to commercial quality. Paddle, ball, bricks - with physics that feel right, power-ups, and full audio.
    69→
    70→**Skills taught:**
    71→- Sub-cell movement (fixed-point pixel precision)
    72→- Ball physics (velocity, angles, reflection, spin)
    73→- Paddle control and angle influence
    74→- Multiple brick types (hard, unbreakable)
    75→- Power-up systems (extend, multi-ball, laser, catch)
    76→- Custom fonts and graphics (UDG)
    77→- Full beeper audio (music + SFX)
    78→- High scores, difficulty settings, multiplayer
    79→- 20 levels of varied brick patterns
    80→
    81→**Why here:** After shooting, physics simulation introduces a fundamentally different movement model. 128 units allows proper exploration of angles, feel, and polish.
    82→
    83→**Detailed outline:** `/docs/curriculum/assembly/sinclair-zx-spectrum/game-02-full-game-outline-zx-spectrum-shatter.md`
    84→
    85→---
    86→
    87→### Game 3: Cavern
    88→**Units:** 128 (8 phases)
    89→**Concept:** Single-screen platformer polished to commercial quality. In the Manic Miner tradition - platforms, hazards, collectibles, air supply, and pixel-perfect jumps.
    90→
    91→**Skills taught:**
    92→- Gravity and jump physics (velocity, acceleration, variable height)
    93→- Platform collision detection (landing, head bonk, walls)
    94→- Player animation (walk cycle, jump, death)
    95→- Patrolling enemies with simple AI
    96→- Crumbling platforms (timed hazards)
    97→- Moving platforms (horizontal and vertical)
    98→- Conveyor belts and environmental hazards
    99→- Air supply mechanic (time pressure)
   100→- Key collection and locked exits
   101→- Power-ups (speed, invincibility)
   102→- 20 levels with progressive difficulty
   103→- Full beeper audio (music and SFX)
   104→
   105→**Why here:** Builds on sub-cell movement from Shatter, applies physics to gravity instead of bouncing. This is the Spectrum's signature genre.
   106→
   107→**Detailed outline:** `/docs/curriculum/assembly/sinclair-zx-spectrum/game-03-full-game-outline-zx-spectrum-cavern.md`
   108→
   109→---
   110→
   111→### Game 4: Chambers
   112→**Units:** 128 (8 phases)
   113→**Concept:** Flip-screen top-down exploration polished to commercial quality. Navigate rooms, collect items, avoid enemies, find the exit. In the Atic Atac tradition - the Spectrum's signature exploration genre.
   114→
   115→**Skills taught:**
   116→- 4-way top-down movement
   117→- Room data structures and transitions
   118→- Persistent state (collected items stay collected)
   119→- Inventory systems (keys, items, weapons)
   120→- Enemy chase AI with wall awareness
   121→- Multiple enemy types (patrol, chase, ranged)
   122→- Coloured keys and locked doors
   123→- Hazards, traps, and environmental puzzles
   124→- Multi-floor world design
   125→- Full beeper audio (music and SFX)
   126→- High scores, difficulty options, map screen
   127→
   128→**Why here:** Multi-room structure and state management prepare learners for more complex AI in following games. 128 units allows full world-building.
   129→
   130→**Detailed outline:** `/docs/curriculum/assembly/sinclair-zx-spectrum/game-04-full-game-outline-zx-spectrum-chambers.md`
   131→
   132→---
   133→
   134→### Game 5: Ink War
   135→**Units:** 128 (8 phases)
   136→**Concept:** Territory control strategy game polished to commercial quality. Player vs AI, taking turns to claim 8×8 attribute cells. Control the majority to win. A unique genre that turns the Spectrum's colour limitations into a feature.
   137→
   138→**Skills taught:**
   139→- Deep attribute system mastery (attribute = gameplay)
   140→- Turn-based game loop (different paradigm)
   141→- Strategic AI (greedy, minimax, personality)
   142→- Position evaluation and scoring
   143→- Multiple game modes (vs AI, two-player)
   144→- Board size variants (6×6, 8×8, 10×10)
   145→- Tournament and handicap modes
   146→- Undo/replay functionality
   147→- Save/load games
   148→- Tutorial mode
   149→- Statistics and achievements
   150→- Full beeper audio (music and effects)
   151→
   152→**Why here:** After 4 action games, learners explore strategic thinking. The attribute system becomes the core mechanic, turning a limitation into innovation.
   153→
   154→**Detailed outline:** `/docs/curriculum/assembly/sinclair-zx-spectrum/game-05-full-game-outline-zx-spectrum-ink-war.md`
   155→
   156→---
   157→
   158→### Game 6: Chomp
   159→**Units:** 128 (8 phases)
   160→**Concept:** Pac-Man style maze game polished to commercial quality. Collect dots, avoid ghosts with distinct AI personalities, eat power pills to turn the tables.
   161→
   162→**Skills taught:**
   163→- Tile-based maze structure
   164→- Constrained corridor movement (only turn at junctions)
   165→- Four ghost AI personalities (Blinky, Pinky, Inky, Clyde)
   166→- Chase/scatter mode state machines
   167→- Frightened mode with ghost-eating chains
   168→- Power-up state transitions
   169→- Screen wrap-around tunnels
   170→- Speed and difficulty progression
   171→- Bonus fruit per level
   172→- Cruise Elroy (Blinky speed-up)
   173→- Intermission cut-scenes
   174→- Full beeper audio (waka-waka, siren, death)
   175→- High scores, demo mode
   176→- Two-player mode
   177→
   178→**Why here:** After strategic Ink War, learners return to real-time action with sophisticated enemy AI. Four distinct ghost behaviours create emergent gameplay.
   179→
   180→**Detailed outline:** `/docs/curriculum/assembly/sinclair-zx-spectrum/game-06-full-game-outline-zx-spectrum-chomp.md`
   181→
   182→---
   183→
   184→### Game 7: Slither
   185→**Units:** 128 (8 phases)
   186→**Concept:** Classic snake game polished to commercial quality. Eat food, grow longer, avoid hitting yourself or walls. Teaches the ring buffer data structure.
   187→
   188→**Skills taught:**
   189→- Ring buffer for body segment storage
   190→- Grid-based movement system
   191→- Self-collision detection algorithms
   192→- Growing entity management
   193→- Food spawning and placement validation
   194→- Speed progression with length
   195→- Multiple maze layouts
   196→- Level system with targets
   197→- Power-up system (slow-down, ghost, shrink)
   198→- Two-player alternating mode
   199→- AI opponent with difficulty levels
   200→- Wrap-around mode option
   201→- Theme system (visual variants)
   202→- Achievement and statistics tracking
   203→- Beeper sound effects and music
   204→- Demo/attract mode
   205→
   206→**Why here:** After maze navigation in Chomp, learners tackle a different grid-based challenge. The ring buffer is a fundamental data structure used throughout game and audio programming.
   207→
   208→**Detailed outline:** `/docs/curriculum/assembly/sinclair-zx-spectrum/game-07-full-game-outline-zx-spectrum-slither.md`
   209→
   210→---
   211→
   212→### Game 8: Stack
   213→**Units:** 128 (8 phases)
   214→**Concept:** Classic falling block puzzle polished to commercial quality. Tetris-style piece rotation, stacking, and line clearing with modern features like wall kicks, T-spins, and competitive multiplayer.
   215→
   216→**Skills taught:**
   217→- Piece rotation mathematics (4 orientations × 7 pieces)
   218→- Timer-based falling mechanics
   219→- Grid collision detection (can piece fit?)
   220→- Line detection and efficient clearing
   221→- Next piece preview and hold piece
   222→- Ghost piece showing landing position
   223→- Wall kick system (SRS-style)
   224→- T-spin detection and bonus scoring
   225→- Combo and back-to-back systems
   226→- 7-bag randomiser for fair distribution
   227→- Speed progression with level system
   228→- Multiple game modes (Marathon, Sprint, Ultra)
   229→- Two-player competitive with garbage lines
   230→- AI opponent with difficulty levels
   231→- Visual themes and customisation
   232→- Achievement and statistics tracking
   233→- Beeper sound effects
   234→- 128K AY music (optional)
   235→- Replay system
   236→- Demo/attract mode
   237→

   551→
   552→## Platform Context
   553→
   554→**Hardware:**
   555→- **CPU:** Zilog Z80 @ 3.5 MHz
   556→- **RAM:** 48K (16K on early models, 128K on later)
   557→- **Display:** 256×192 pixels, 32×24 attribute cells
   558→- **Colours:** 8 colours, each with normal/bright variant (15 total + black)
   559→- **Sound:** 1-bit beeper (128K models add AY-3-8912 chip)
   560→- **Storage:** Cassette tape (+3 models have disk)
   561→
   562→**The Attribute System:**
   563→The Spectrum's display divides the screen into 8×8 pixel cells. Each cell has one attribute byte controlling:
   564→- INK (foreground colour, 3 bits)
   565→- PAPER (background colour, 3 bits)
   566→- BRIGHT (intensity, 1 bit)
   567→- FLASH (alternating, 1 bit)
   568→
   569→This creates the infamous "colour clash" when differently-coloured objects overlap within an 8×8 cell. Managing this constraint is central to Spectrum development.
   570→
   571→**No Hardware Sprites:**
   572→Unlike the C64, the Spectrum has no hardware sprites. All moving graphics are software-rendered, making efficient drawing routines essential.
   573→
   574→**No Hardware Scrolling:**
   575→Smooth scrolling requires shifting screen memory in software - an expensive operation that defined what was possible on the platform.
   576→

1→# Nintendo NES Curriculum
     2→
     3→**Platform:** Nintendo Entertainment System (NES/Famicom)
     4→**Language:** 6502 Assembly (no BASIC gateway)
     5→**Killer Feature:** PPU (Picture Processing Unit) - tile-based graphics mastery
     6→**Games:** 16
     7→**Total Units:** 3,584
     8→
     9→---
    10→
    11→## Overview
    12→
    13→The NES curriculum teaches 6502 assembly programming through 16 complete games. The sequence begins with the PPU - the heart of NES development - and progresses through scrolling, Metroidvania design, and advanced mapper techniques until learners can produce commercial-quality cartridge games.
    14→
    15→The NES is fundamentally tile-based. Everything is 8×8 tiles: backgrounds built from pattern tables arranged in nametables, sprites limited to 64 objects with 8 per scanline. Understanding the PPU is understanding the NES. This curriculum embraces that architecture, teaching learners to think in tiles.
    16→
    17→By the final game, learners will have skills matching professional NES developers of the late 1980s and early 1990s.
    18→
    19→---
    20→
    21→## Curriculum Structure
    22→
    23→| Phase | Games | Units Each | Total |
    24→|-------|-------|------------|-------|
    25→| Foundation (platformer, platformer, breakout, maze, exploration, snake, puzzle, scrolling) | 1-8 | 128 | 1,024 |
    26→| Intermediate | 9-10 | 256 | 512 |
    27→| Advanced | 11-13 | 256 | 768 |
    28→| Advanced | 14 | 256 | 256 |
    29→| Expert | 15 | 512 | 512 |
    30→| Capstone | 16 | 512 | 512 |
    31→| **Total** | **16** | | **3,584** |
    32→
    33→Each game is divided into 4 phases (foundation games) or 8 phases (advanced/Games 1-2) or 16 phases (capstone), with each phase containing 16 units.
    34→
    35→---
    36→
    37→## Game Sequence
    38→
    39→### Foundation Games
    40→
    41→#### Game 1: Dash
    42→**Units:** 128 (8 phases)
    43→**Concept:** Side-scrolling platformer. Run, jump, and collect your way through tile-based levels. The NES was built for this — the PPU's nametables, hardware scrolling, and OAM sprites all come together naturally in a platformer.
    44→
    45→**Skills taught:**
    46→- PPU setup (pattern tables, nametables, palettes)
    47→- Background tiles (level layout via nametables)
    48→- Sprites via OAM (player character, collectibles, enemies)
    49→- Controller input (D-pad movement, A to jump)
    50→- Gravity and jump physics (velocity, acceleration, variable height)
    51→- Tile-based collision detection
    52→- Platform types (solid, one-way, moving)
    53→- NES hardware scrolling (PPUSCROLL)
    54→- Camera following with nametable management
    55→- Collectibles and score tracking
    56→- Hazards and enemy patterns
    57→- APU sound basics
    58→- Player animation states (idle, run, jump, fall)
    59→- Level data structures and metatiles
    60→
    61→**Why first:** The NES is the platformer machine — Super Mario Bros. defined the console. A platformer teaches the PPU through its most natural use: tiles become platforms, sprites become characters, scrolling becomes the world opening up. Learners see a character on screen by Unit 2, jump by Unit 3, and the screen scrolls by mid-game.
    62→
    63→**Detailed outline:** `/docs/curriculum/assembly/nintendo-nes/game-01-full-game-outline-nes-dash.md`
    64→
    65→---
    66→
    67→#### Game 2: Crate Escape
    68→**Units:** 128 (8 phases)
    69→**Concept:** Single-screen platformer polished to commercial quality. Jump between platforms, avoid hazards, collect items, defeat enemies, reach the exit.
    70→
    71→**Skills taught:**
    72→- Gravity and jump physics (with variable height, coyote time)
    73→- Tile-based collision (the NES way)
    74→- Metatile level design
    75→- Player animation states
    76→- Hazards (spikes, enemies, conveyors, collapsing platforms)
    77→- Moving platforms (horizontal, vertical)
    78→- Ladders and collectibles
    79→- Full APU music and sound
    80→- High scores, passwords, boss level
    81→- 20 levels of varied design
    82→
    83→**Why here:** After shooting, platforming introduces gravity and tile collision - fundamental NES skills. 128 units allows proper exploration of feel, level design, and polish.
    84→
    85→**Detailed outline:** `/docs/curriculum/assembly/nintendo-nes/game-02-full-game-outline-nes-crate-escape.md`
    86→
    87→---
    88→
    89→#### Game 3: Fracture Point
    90→**Units:** 128 (8 phases)
    91→**Concept:** Breakout clone polished to commercial quality. Paddle, ball, bricks - with fixed-point physics, power-ups, and 20 varied levels. The tension builds to the fracture point.
    92→
    93→**Skills taught:**
    94→- Ball physics with fixed-point maths (smooth sub-pixel movement)

   553→---
   554→
   555→## Platform Context
   556→
   557→### Hardware
   558→
   559→- **CPU:** Ricoh 2A03 (MOS 6502 core) @ 1.79 MHz (NTSC) / 1.66 MHz (PAL)
   560→- **RAM:** 2KB work RAM, 2KB video RAM
   561→- **Display:** 256×240 pixels (NTSC), 256×224 visible
   562→- **Colours:** 52 colours in palette, 25 on-screen (4 background palettes + 4 sprite palettes, 4 colours each including shared background)
   563→- **Sound:** APU - 2 pulse, 1 triangle, 1 noise, 1 DMC (sample) channel
   564→- **Storage:** Cartridge with ROM, optional battery backup
   565→
   566→### The PPU (Picture Processing Unit)
   567→
   568→The heart of NES graphics:
   569→
   570→- **Pattern Tables** - Two 4KB tables holding 8×8 pixel tile definitions (256 tiles each for background and sprites)
   571→- **Nametables** - Four 1KB screens defining which tiles appear where (32×30 tiles visible)
   572→- **Attribute Tables** - Define which palette each 16×16 pixel area uses
   573→- **OAM (Object Attribute Memory)** - 256 bytes defining 64 sprites (position, tile, palette, flip)
   574→
   575→### The 8-Sprite Limit
   576→
   577→Only 8 sprites can appear on a single scanline. More causes flickering. Managing this limit is a core NES skill.
   578→
   579→### The APU (Audio Processing Unit)
   580→
   581→Five channels of distinctive 8-bit sound:
   582→- **Pulse 1 & 2** - Square waves with duty cycle control (melodies, harmony)
   583→- **Triangle** - Pure triangle wave (bass, smooth tones)
   584→- **Noise** - White noise generator (drums, percussion, effects)
   585→- **DMC** - Delta modulation for samples (voice, complex sounds)
   586→
   587→### Mappers
   588→
   589→Bank-switching hardware on cartridges enabling larger games:
   590→- **NROM** - No mapper, 32KB PRG + 8KB CHR (early games)
   591→- **MMC1** - Bank switching up to 256KB PRG (Zelda, Metroid)
   592→- **MMC3** - Advanced features, scanline counter (Super Mario Bros. 3)
   593→
   594→The curriculum progresses from NROM-compatible basics to MMC3 mastery.
   595→
