1→# Sinclair ZX Spectrum Curriculum
     2→
     3→**Platform:** Sinclair ZX Spectrum
     4→**Killer Feature:** Attribute colour system
     5→**Games:** 16
     6→**Total Units:** 3,584
     7→
     8→---
     9→
    10→## Overview
    11→
    12→The ZX Spectrum curriculum teaches Z80 assembly programming through 16 complete games. The sequence begins with the attribute system - the Spectrum's defining characteristic that created its distinctive visual style - and progresses through scrolling, enemy AI, isometric projection, and advanced techniques until learners can produce commercial-quality games.
    13→
    14→The Spectrum had no hardware sprites, no smooth scrolling, and a single-bit beeper for sound. Every impressive effect was achieved through software and creativity. This curriculum embraces those constraints, teaching learners to make the hardware sing through ingenuity.
    15→
    16→By the final game, learners will have skills matching professional Spectrum developers of the late 1980s - equivalent to teams at Ultimate, Ocean, or Codemasters.
    17→
    18→---
    19→
    20→## Unit Structure
    21→
    22→Games scale in unit count based on complexity:
    23→
    24→| Games | Units Each | Total | Rationale |
    25→|-------|-----------|-------|-----------|
    26→| 1-8 | 128 | 1,024 | Foundation games - maze explorer, physics, platformer, exploration, strategy, maze, snake, Tetris |
    27→| 9-10 | 256 | 512 | Intermediate - scrolling, character-based display mastery |
    28→| 11-13 | 256 | 768 | System-heavy - multiple complex systems |
    29→| 14 | 256 | 256 | Advanced - Filmation-style isometric (Head Over Heels) |
    30→| 15 | 512 | 512 | Expert - Knight Lore-style isometric with transformation |
    31→| 16 | 512 | 512 | Capstone - commercial quality with 128K/48K dual support |
    32→| **Total** | | **3,584** | |
    33→
    34→Each game follows a phase structure (4-16 phases depending on length), with each phase containing 16 units.
    35→
    36→---
    37→
    38→## Game Sequence
    39→
    40→### Game 1: Shadowkeep
    41→**Units:** 128 (8 phases)
    42→**Concept:** Top-down maze explorer. Navigate rooms of a dark keep, collect keys, avoid creatures, find the exit. The attribute system is the game — ink and paper define walls, floors, items, and danger.
    43→
    44→**Skills taught:**
    45→- Screen memory layout ($4000-$57FF)
    46→- Attribute memory ($5800-$5AFF)
    47→- INK, PAPER, BRIGHT, FLASH bits
    48→- Keyboard input via ROM routines
    49→- Character-cell movement (8-pixel grid)
    50→- Wall collision via attribute reading
    51→- Room data structures (tile maps)
    52→- Item collection and inventory
    53→- Enemy patrol AI (simple path following)
    54→- Beeper sound effects (pitch variation)
    55→- Multi-room transitions
    56→- Key and door mechanics
    57→- Score and status display
    58→- Increasing difficulty (more rooms, faster enemies)
    59→
    60→**Why first:** A maze explorer makes the attribute system the core mechanic from Unit 1. Walls are INK, floors are PAPER, items are BRIGHT — the Spectrum's defining feature becomes the game design tool. Learners see pixels on screen immediately and navigate a room by Unit 3.
    61→
    62→**Detailed outline:** `/docs/curriculum/assembly/sinclair-zx-spectrum/game-01-full-game-outline-zx-spectrum-shadowkeep.md`
    63→
    64→---
    65→
    66→### Game 2: Shatter
    67→**Units:** 128 (8 phases)
    68→**Concept:** Breakout clone polished to commercial quality. Paddle, ball, bricks - with physics that feel right, power-ups, and full audio.
    69→
    70→**Skills taught:**
    71→- Sub-cell movement (fixed-point pixel precision)
    72→- Ball physics (velocity, angles, reflection, spin)
    73→- Paddle control and angle influence
    74→- Multiple brick types (hard, unbreakable)
    75→- Power-up systems (extend, multi-ball, laser, catch)
    76→- Custom fonts and graphics (UDG)
    77→- Full beeper audio (music + SFX)
    78→- High scores, difficulty settings, multiplayer
    79→- 20 levels of varied brick patterns
    80→
    81→**Why here:** After shooting, physics simulation introduces a fundamentally different movement model. 128 units allows proper exploration of angles, feel, and polish.
    82→
    83→**Detailed outline:** `/docs/curriculum/assembly/sinclair-zx-spectrum/game-02-full-game-outline-zx-spectrum-shatter.md`
    84→
    85→---
    86→
    87→### Game 3: Cavern
    88→**Units:** 128 (8 phases)
    89→**Concept:** Single-screen platformer polished to commercial quality. In the Manic Miner tradition - platforms, hazards, collectibles, air supply, and pixel-perfect jumps.
    90→
    91→**Skills taught:**
    92→- Gravity and jump physics (velocity, acceleration, variable height)
    93→- Platform collision detection (landing, head bonk, walls)
    94→- Player animation (walk cycle, jump, death)
    95→- Patrolling enemies with simple AI
    96→- Crumbling platforms (timed hazards)
    97→- Moving platforms (horizontal and vertical)
    98→- Conveyor belts and environmental hazards
    99→- Air supply mechanic (time pressure)
   100→- Key collection and locked exits
   101→- Power-ups (speed, invincibility)
   102→- 20 levels with progressive difficulty
   103→- Full beeper audio (music and SFX)
   104→
   105→**Why here:** Builds on sub-cell movement from Shatter, applies physics to gravity instead of bouncing. This is the Spectrum's signature genre.
   106→
   107→**Detailed outline:** `/docs/curriculum/assembly/sinclair-zx-spectrum/game-03-full-game-outline-zx-spectrum-cavern.md`
   108→
   109→---
   110→
   111→### Game 4: Chambers
   112→**Units:** 128 (8 phases)
   113→**Concept:** Flip-screen top-down exploration polished to commercial quality. Navigate rooms, collect items, avoid enemies, find the exit. In the Atic Atac tradition - the Spectrum's signature exploration genre.
   114→
   115→**Skills taught:**
   116→- 4-way top-down movement
   117→- Room data structures and transitions
   118→- Persistent state (collected items stay collected)
   119→- Inventory systems (keys, items, weapons)
   120→- Enemy chase AI with wall awareness
   121→- Multiple enemy types (patrol, chase, ranged)
   122→- Coloured keys and locked doors
   123→- Hazards, traps, and environmental puzzles
   124→- Multi-floor world design
   125→- Full beeper audio (music and SFX)
   126→- High scores, difficulty options, map screen
   127→
   128→**Why here:** Multi-room structure and state management prepare learners for more complex AI in following games. 128 units allows full world-building.
   129→
   130→**Detailed outline:** `/docs/curriculum/assembly/sinclair-zx-spectrum/game-04-full-game-outline-zx-spectrum-chambers.md`
   131→
   132→---
   133→
   134→### Game 5: Ink War
   135→**Units:** 128 (8 phases)
   136→**Concept:** Territory control strategy game polished to commercial quality. Player vs AI, taking turns to claim 8×8 attribute cells. Control the majority to win. A unique genre that turns the Spectrum's colour limitations into a feature.
   137→
   138→**Skills taught:**
   139→- Deep attribute system mastery (attribute = gameplay)
   140→- Turn-based game loop (different paradigm)
   141→- Strategic AI (greedy, minimax, personality)
   142→- Position evaluation and scoring
   143→- Multiple game modes (vs AI, two-player)
   144→- Board size variants (6×6, 8×8, 10×10)
   145→- Tournament and handicap modes
   146→- Undo/replay functionality
   147→- Save/load games
   148→- Tutorial mode
   149→- Statistics and achievements
   150→- Full beeper audio (music and effects)
   151→
   152→**Why here:** After 4 action games, learners explore strategic thinking. The attribute system becomes the core mechanic, turning a limitation into innovation.
   153→
   154→**Detailed outline:** `/docs/curriculum/assembly/sinclair-zx-spectrum/game-05-full-game-outline-zx-spectrum-ink-war.md`
   155→
   156→---
   157→
   158→### Game 6: Chomp
   159→**Units:** 128 (8 phases)
   160→**Concept:** Pac-Man style maze game polished to commercial quality. Collect dots, avoid ghosts with distinct AI personalities, eat power pills to turn the tables.
   161→
   162→**Skills taught:**
   163→- Tile-based maze structure
   164→- Constrained corridor movement (only turn at junctions)
   165→- Four ghost AI personalities (Blinky, Pinky, Inky, Clyde)
   166→- Chase/scatter mode state machines
   167→- Frightened mode with ghost-eating chains
   168→- Power-up state transitions
   169→- Screen wrap-around tunnels
   170→- Speed and difficulty progression
   171→- Bonus fruit per level
   172→- Cruise Elroy (Blinky speed-up)
   173→- Intermission cut-scenes
   174→- Full beeper audio (waka-waka, siren, death)
   175→- High scores, demo mode
   176→- Two-player mode
   177→
   178→**Why here:** After strategic Ink War, learners return to real-time action with sophisticated enemy AI. Four distinct ghost behaviours create emergent gameplay.
   179→
   180→**Detailed outline:** `/docs/curriculum/assembly/sinclair-zx-spectrum/game-06-full-game-outline-zx-spectrum-chomp.md`
   181→
   182→---
   183→
   184→### Game 7: Slither
   185→**Units:** 128 (8 phases)
   186→**Concept:** Classic snake game polished to commercial quality. Eat food, grow longer, avoid hitting yourself or walls. Teaches the ring buffer data structure.
   187→
   188→**Skills taught:**
   189→- Ring buffer for body segment storage
   190→- Grid-based movement system
   191→- Self-collision detection algorithms
   192→- Growing entity management
   193→- Food spawning and placement validation
   194→- Speed progression with length
   195→- Multiple maze layouts
   196→- Level system with targets
   197→- Power-up system (slow-down, ghost, shrink)
   198→- Two-player alternating mode
   199→- AI opponent with difficulty levels
   200→- Wrap-around mode option
   201→- Theme system (visual variants)
   202→- Achievement and statistics tracking
   203→- Beeper sound effects and music
   204→- Demo/attract mode
   205→
   206→**Why here:** After maze navigation in Chomp, learners tackle a different grid-based challenge. The ring buffer is a fundamental data structure used throughout game and audio programming.
   207→
   208→**Detailed outline:** `/docs/curriculum/assembly/sinclair-zx-spectrum/game-07-full-game-outline-zx-spectrum-slither.md`
   209→
   210→---
   211→
   212→### Game 8: Stack
   213→**Units:** 128 (8 phases)
   214→**Concept:** Classic falling block puzzle polished to commercial quality. Tetris-style piece rotation, stacking, and line clearing with modern features like wall kicks, T-spins, and competitive multiplayer.
   215→
   216→**Skills taught:**
   217→- Piece rotation mathematics (4 orientations × 7 pieces)
   218→- Timer-based falling mechanics
   219→- Grid collision detection (can piece fit?)
   220→- Line detection and efficient clearing
   221→- Next piece preview and hold piece
   222→- Ghost piece showing landing position
   223→- Wall kick system (SRS-style)
   224→- T-spin detection and bonus scoring
   225→- Combo and back-to-back systems
   226→- 7-bag randomiser for fair distribution
   227→- Speed progression with level system
   228→- Multiple game modes (Marathon, Sprint, Ultra)
   229→- Two-player competitive with garbage lines
   230→- AI opponent with difficulty levels
   231→- Visual themes and customisation
   232→- Achievement and statistics tracking
   233→- Beeper sound effects
   234→- 128K AY music (optional)
   235→- Replay system
   236→- Demo/attract mode
   237→
   238→**Why here:** The falling blocks puzzle teaches rotation mathematics, grid algorithms, and polished game feel. Different from snake's ring buffer, this focuses on piece manipulation and clearing mechanics.
   239→
   240→**Detailed outline:** `/docs/curriculum/assembly/sinclair-zx-spectrum/game-08-full-game-outline-zx-spectrum-stack.md`
   241→
   242→---
   243→
   244→### Game 9: Blitz
   245→**Units:** 256 (16 phases)
   246→**Concept:** Side-scrolling shooter on hardware with NO scroll support. Master THE hardest Spectrum technique: software pixel scrolling. Shift every byte, manage contended memory timing, and build a complete R-Type style shooter.
   247→
   248→**Skills taught:**
   249→- Software horizontal scrolling (character and pixel-level)
   250→- Stack-based scroll tricks for maximum speed
   251→- Unrolled and self-modifying scroll routines
   252→- Contended memory timing management
   253→- Attribute clash handling during scroll
   254→- Tile map level streaming
   255→- Parallax pseudo-layers via colour tricks
   256→- Player and enemies in scrolling playfield
   257→- Complex bullet patterns and enemy AI
   258→- Five complete levels with distinct themes
   259→- Five epic boss battles
   260→- Weapon upgrade system with multiple types
   261→- 128K AY sound enhancements
   262→- Multiple game modes (Normal, Boss Rush, Endless)
   263→- Two-player alternating mode
   264→- Comprehensive optimisation techniques
   265→
   266→**Why expanded:** Software scrolling is THE hardest Spectrum technique. Games like R-Type achieved legendary status for smooth scrolling on hardware with no scroll support. 256 units allows mastery of this platform-defining challenge.
   267→
   268→**Detailed outline:** `/docs/curriculum/assembly/sinclair-zx-spectrum/game-09-full-game-outline-zx-spectrum-blitz.md`
   269→
   270→---
   271→
   272→### Game 10: Expanse
   273→**Units:** 256 (16 phases)
   274→**Concept:** Epic scrolling platformer combining Blitz's scrolling mastery with full platformer mechanics. Vast worlds to explore with multiple themed areas, bosses, and commercial-quality polish.
   275→
   276→**Skills taught:**
   277→- Software scrolling + platformer physics integration
   278→- Camera systems with dead zones
   279→- Level compression and streaming
   280→- Scrolling collision detection
   281→- Slope physics
   282→- Moving platforms
   283→- Full enemy system in scrolling world
   284→- Four themed worlds with unique graphics
   285→- Boss encounters per world
   286→- Health, lives, and checkpoint systems
   287→- Collectibles and power-ups
   288→- AY-3-8910 music and effects
   289→- Save/password systems
   290→- Multiple game modes
   291→- 48K and 128K versions
   292→
   293→**Why expanded:** Combining scrolling and platforming is the ultimate Spectrum challenge. 256 units allows a complete, polished scrolling platformer worthy of commercial release.
   294→
   295→**Detailed outline:** `/docs/curriculum/assembly/sinclair-zx-spectrum/game-10-full-game-outline-zx-spectrum-expanse.md`
   296→
   297→---
   298→
   299→### Game 11: Redline
   300→**Units:** 256 (16 phases)
   301→**Concept:** Championship vertical scrolling racer with multiple tracks, AI opponents, weather effects, and damage system. Introduces tape loading fundamentals.
   302→
   303→**Skills taught:**
   304→- Vertical software scrolling
   305→- Racing physics (acceleration, braking, grip)
   306→- Road rendering with curves and hills
   307→- Multiple AI opponents with overtaking logic
   308→- Rubber banding and difficulty scaling
   309→- Collision and damage system
   310→- Pit stop mechanics
   311→- 8 unique tracks (city, coast, mountain, night)
   312→- Championship mode with points
   313→- Weather effects (rain, night)
   314→- Dynamic weather transitions
   315→- **Standard tape loading routines**
   316→- **TAP file format and creation**
   317→- **Multi-load structure**
   318→- Time trial and qualifying modes
   319→- Two-player mode
   320→
   321→**Why expanded:** A full championship racer with AI, weather, damage, pit stops, AND tape loading fundamentals requires 256 units.
   322→
   323→**Detailed outline:** `/docs/curriculum/assembly/sinclair-zx-spectrum/game-11-full-game-outline-zx-spectrum-redline.md`
   324→
   325→**Detailed outline:** `/docs/plans/sinclair-zx-spectrum/game-11-redline.md`
   326→
   327→---
   328→
   329→### Game 12: Recoil
   330→**Units:** 256 (16 phases)
   331→**Concept:** Contra-style run 'n' gun action with 8-way shooting, multiple weapons, and epic boss battles. Two-player co-op and intense action on the Spectrum.
   332→
   333→**Skills taught:**

   551→
   552→## Platform Context
   553→
   554→**Hardware:**
   555→- **CPU:** Zilog Z80 @ 3.5 MHz
   556→- **RAM:** 48K (16K on early models, 128K on later)
   557→- **Display:** 256×192 pixels, 32×24 attribute cells
   558→- **Colours:** 8 colours, each with normal/bright variant (15 total + black)
   559→- **Sound:** 1-bit beeper (128K models add AY-3-8912 chip)
   560→- **Storage:** Cassette tape (+3 models have disk)
   561→
   562→**The Attribute System:**
   563→The Spectrum's display divides the screen into 8×8 pixel cells. Each cell has one attribute byte controlling:
   564→- INK (foreground colour, 3 bits)
   565→- PAPER (background colour, 3 bits)
   566→- BRIGHT (intensity, 1 bit)
   567→- FLASH (alternating, 1 bit)
   568→
   569→This creates the infamous "colour clash" when differently-coloured objects overlap within an 8×8 cell. Managing this constraint is central to Spectrum development.
   570→
   571→**No Hardware Sprites:**
   572→Unlike the C64, the Spectrum has no hardware sprites. All moving graphics are software-rendered, making efficient drawing routines essential.
   573→
   574→**No Hardware Scrolling:**
   575→Smooth scrolling requires shifting screen memory in software - an expensive operation that defined what was possible on the platform.
   576→

1→# Commodore 64 Curriculum
     2→
     3→**Platform:** Commodore 64
     4→**Killer Feature:** SID sound chip
     5→**Games:** 16
     6→**Total Units:** 3,584
     7→
     8→---
     9→
    10→## Overview
    11→
    12→The Commodore 64 curriculum teaches 6510 assembly programming through 16 complete games. The sequence begins with the SID chip - the C64's defining feature - and progresses through sprites, scrolling, raster effects, and advanced techniques until learners can produce commercial-quality games.
    13→
    14→By the final game, learners will have skills matching professional C64 developers of the late 1980s and early 1990s - equivalent to teams at Factor 5, System 3, or Thalamus.
    15→
    16→---
    17→
    18→## Unit Structure
    19→
    20→Games scale in unit count based on complexity:
    21→
    22→| Games | Units Each | Total | Rationale |
    23→|-------|-----------|-------|-----------|
    24→| 1-8 | 128 | 1,024 | Foundation games - shooter, platformer, maze, breakout, exploration, rhythm, scrolling, Scramble |
    25→| 9-10 | 256 | 512 | Intermediate - sprite multiplexing, pseudo-3D racing |
    26→| 11-13 | 256 | 768 | System-heavy - multiple complex systems |
    27→| 14 | 256 | 256 | Advanced - isometric paradigm (Knight Lore-style) |
    28→| 15 | 512 | 512 | Expert - Turrican-style run'n'gun masterpiece |
    29→| 16 | 512 | 512 | Capstone - commercial quality with demo-scene techniques |
    30→| **Total** | | **3,584** | |
    31→
    32→Each game follows a phase structure (4-16 phases depending on length), with each phase containing 16 units.
    33→

    36→## Game Sequence

    38→### Game 1: Starfield

    41→
    42→**Skills taught:**
    43→- Hardware sprites (enabling, positioning, pointers)
    44→- Joystick input via CIA
    45→- Sprite-to-sprite collision
    46→- Bullet management and pooling
    47→- Score display
    48→- Wave systems
    49→- Power-ups and ship upgrades
    50→- SID sound effects (integrated throughout)
    51→- Multicolour sprites
    52→- Boss battles
    53→- Commercial-quality polish
    54→
    55→**Why first:** A shooter gets something playable on screen immediately. Learners control a ship from Unit 2, shoot from Unit 3. The SID is introduced through satisfying sound effects, not theory.
    56→
    57→**Detailed outline:** `/docs/curriculum/assembly/commodore-64/game-01-full-game-outline-c64-starfield.md`

   197→### Game 8: Night Raid
   198→**Units:** 128 (8 phases)
   199→**Concept:** Scramble-style side-scroller polished to commercial quality. Fly your jet through enemy territory, navigate scrolling terrain, shoot enemies, bomb ground targets, and manage fuel. A complete horizontal shooter showcasing combined scrolling and combat mechanics.
   200→
   201→**Skills taught:**
   202→- Scrolling combined with shooting mechanics
   203→- Character-based terrain collision in scrolling world
   204→- Resource management (fuel depletion and collection)
   205→- Dual weapon systems (forward shots and bombs)
   206→- Ground targets and air enemies
   207→- Level streaming from compressed data
   208→- Multiple enemy types with varied patterns
   209→- Boss encounters with pattern-based attacks
   210→- Power-up system (speed, rapid fire, shield, weapon upgrades)
   211→- Raster colour effects for atmosphere
   212→- Parallax starfield background
   213→- Multiple levels with themed terrain
   214→- Checkpoint and lives system
   215→- Two-player alternating mode
   216→- Demo/attract mode
   217→- Turbo loader for fast loading
   218→
   219→**Why here:** Building on Scroll Runner's horizontal scrolling foundation, Night Raid combines scrolling with the shooting mechanics learned in earlier games. Resource management adds strategic depth.
   220→
   221→**Detailed outline:** `/docs/curriculum/assembly/commodore-64/game-08-full-game-outline-c64-night-raid.md`

   225→### Game 9: Sprite Storm
   226→**Units:** 256 (16 phases)
   227→**Concept:** Arena shooter displaying 24-32 sprites using THE defining C64 technique: sprite multiplexing. Master raster interrupt timing, Y-sorted sprite allocation, and cycle-exact code to overcome the 8-sprite hardware limit.
   228→
   229→**Skills taught:**
   230→- Sprite multiplexing (8→32 sprites)
   231→- Y-sorted sprite lists with bucket sort
   232→- Raster interrupt timing and chaining
   233→- Double IRQ for stable raster
   234→- Cycle-exact timing and badline awareness
   235→- Self-modifying IRQ handlers
   236→- Flicker management and priority systems
   237→- Sprite crunch bug avoidance
   238→- Arena shooter with wave-based spawning
   239→- Multi-sprite composite bosses
   240→- Multiple enemy AI types
   241→- Weapon upgrade systems
   242→- Two-player co-op and versus modes
   243→- SID music during heavy IRQ load
   244→- Performance profiling and optimisation
   245→
   246→**Why expanded:** Sprite multiplexing is THE advanced C64 technique. Games like Katakis, Turrican, and Armalyte relied on it. 256 units allows mastery of raster timing, sorted allocation, and flicker-free display.
   247→
   248→**Detailed outline:** `/docs/curriculum/assembly/commodore-64/game-09-full-game-outline-c64-sprite-storm.md`

   449→## BASIC V2 Gateway (Optional)
   450→
   451→For learners wanting a gentler introduction before assembly, an optional BASIC V2 curriculum is available. This 512-unit track (8 games × 64 units) teaches programming fundamentals through complete games before tackling 6510.
   452→
   453→**Games:** Number Hunter, Cosmic Drift, Catcher, Maze Escape, Snake Trail, Space Dodge, Memory Match, Platform Peril

   455→**Skills taught:**
   456→- BASIC V2 fundamentals (PRINT, INPUT, IF/THEN)
   457→- POKE/PEEK for screen and colour memory
   458→- Character graphics and custom characters
   459→- Real-time input with GET
   460→- Arrays and game state
   461→- BASIC's limits - why assembly is needed
   462→
   463→The BASIC gateway is optional. Learners can start directly at Game 1 (SID Symphony) with no BASIC knowledge required. The gateway exists for those who prefer building foundational programming concepts before diving into assembly.

   465→See `/docs/curriculum/commodore-64-basic-curriculum.md` for full details.

1→# Nintendo NES Curriculum
     2→
     3→**Platform:** Nintendo Entertainment System (NES/Famicom)
     4→**Language:** 6502 Assembly (no BASIC gateway)
     5→**Killer Feature:** PPU (Picture Processing Unit) - tile-based graphics mastery
     6→**Games:** 16
     7→**Total Units:** 3,584
     8→
     9→---
    10→
    11→## Overview
    12→
    13→The NES curriculum teaches 6502 assembly programming through 16 complete games. The sequence begins with the PPU - the heart of NES development - and progresses through scrolling, Metroidvania design, and advanced mapper techniques until learners can produce commercial-quality cartridge games.
    14→
    15→The NES is fundamentally tile-based. Everything is 8×8 tiles: backgrounds built from pattern tables arranged in nametables, sprites limited to 64 objects with 8 per scanline. Understanding the PPU is understanding the NES. This curriculum embraces that architecture, teaching learners to think in tiles.
    16→
    17→By the final game, learners will have skills matching professional NES developers of the late 1980s and early 1990s.
    18→
    19→---
    20→
    21→## Curriculum Structure
    22→
    23→| Phase | Games | Units Each | Total |
    24→|-------|-------|------------|-------|
    25→| Foundation (platformer, platformer, breakout, maze, exploration, snake, puzzle, scrolling) | 1-8 | 128 | 1,024 |
    26→| Intermediate | 9-10 | 256 | 512 |
    27→| Advanced | 11-13 | 256 | 768 |
    28→| Advanced | 14 | 256 | 256 |
    29→| Expert | 15 | 512 | 512 |
    30→| Capstone | 16 | 512 | 512 |
    31→| **Total** | **16** | | **3,584** |
    32→
    33→Each game is divided into 4 phases (foundation games) or 8 phases (advanced/Games 1-2) or 16 phases (capstone), with each phase containing 16 units.
    34→
    35→---
    36→
    37→## Game Sequence
    38→
    39→### Foundation Games
    40→
    41→#### Game 1: Dash
    42→**Units:** 128 (8 phases)
    43→**Concept:** Side-scrolling platformer. Run, jump, and collect your way through tile-based levels. The NES was built for this — the PPU's nametables, hardware scrolling, and OAM sprites all come together naturally in a platformer.
    44→
    45→**Skills taught:**
    46→- PPU setup (pattern tables, nametables, palettes)
    47→- Background tiles (level layout via nametables)
    48→- Sprites via OAM (player character, collectibles, enemies)
    49→- Controller input (D-pad movement, A to jump)
    50→- Gravity and jump physics (velocity, acceleration, variable height)
    51→- Tile-based collision detection
    52→- Platform types (solid, one-way, moving)
    53→- NES hardware scrolling (PPUSCROLL)
    54→- Camera following with nametable management
    55→- Collectibles and score tracking
    56→- Hazards and enemy patterns
    57→- APU sound basics
    58→- Player animation states (idle, run, jump, fall)
    59→- Level data structures and metatiles
    60→
    61→**Why first:** The NES is the platformer machine — Super Mario Bros. defined the console. A platformer teaches the PPU through its most natural use: tiles become platforms, sprites become characters, scrolling becomes the world opening up. Learners see a character on screen by Unit 2, jump by Unit 3, and the screen scrolls by mid-game.
    62→
    63→**Detailed outline:** `/docs/curriculum/assembly/nintendo-nes/game-01-full-game-outline-nes-dash.md`
    64→
    65→---
    66→
    67→#### Game 2: Crate Escape
    68→**Units:** 128 (8 phases)
    69→**Concept:** Single-screen platformer polished to commercial quality. Jump between platforms, avoid hazards, collect items, defeat enemies, reach the exit.
    70→
    71→**Skills taught:**
    72→- Gravity and jump physics (with variable height, coyote time)
    73→- Tile-based collision (the NES way)
    74→- Metatile level design
    75→- Player animation states
    76→- Hazards (spikes, enemies, conveyors, collapsing platforms)
    77→- Moving platforms (horizontal, vertical)
    78→- Ladders and collectibles
    79→- Full APU music and sound
    80→- High scores, passwords, boss level
    81→- 20 levels of varied design
    82→
    83→**Why here:** After shooting, platforming introduces gravity and tile collision - fundamental NES skills. 128 units allows proper exploration of feel, level design, and polish.
    84→
    85→**Detailed outline:** `/docs/curriculum/assembly/nintendo-nes/game-02-full-game-outline-nes-crate-escape.md`
    86→
    87→---
    88→
    89→#### Game 3: Fracture Point
    90→**Units:** 128 (8 phases)
    91→**Concept:** Breakout clone polished to commercial quality. Paddle, ball, bricks - with fixed-point physics, power-ups, and 20 varied levels. The tension builds to the fracture point.
    92→
    93→**Skills taught:**
    94→- Ball physics with fixed-point maths (smooth sub-pixel movement)
    95→- Background tile modification (bricks disappear from nametable)
    96→- Paddle angle control (hit position affects bounce)
    97→- Multiple brick types (hard, unbreakable, explosive)
    98→- Power-up system (extend, multi-ball, laser, catch)
    99→- APU sound design for impacts
   100→- Level design variety
   101→- Attribute table for brick colours
   102→- Score multipliers and combos
   103→- Full polish (20 levels, music, high scores)
   104→
   105→**Why here:** After sprites in Games 1-2, learners master dynamic backgrounds. Ball physics teaches fixed-point maths before applying it to more complex games.
   106→
   107→**Detailed outline:** `/docs/curriculum/assembly/nintendo-nes/game-03-full-game-outline-nes-fracture-point.md`
   108→
   109→---
   110→
   111→#### Game 4: Pellet Panic
   112→**Units:** 128 (8 phases)
   113→**Concept:** Pac-Man style maze game polished to commercial quality. Collect all pellets, avoid ghosts, grab power pills to turn the tables. Frantic, panic-inducing energy with four distinct ghost personalities.
   114→
   115→**Skills taught:**
   116→- Tile-based maze structure (background tiles)
   117→- Constrained corridor movement with smooth cornering
   118→- Ghost AI personalities (Blinky chase, Pinky ambush, Inky flank, Clyde random)
   119→- Chase/scatter mode switching on timers
   120→- Frightened mode with ghost-eating chains
   121→- Power-up state changes
   122→- Screen wrap-around tunnels
   123→- Speed and difficulty progression
   124→- Bonus fruit per level
   125→- Full APU audio (waka-waka, siren, death)
   126→- High scores, demo mode, intermissions
   127→
   128→**Why here:** After physics games, AI personalities bring the maze to life. 128 units allows four distinct ghost behaviours.
   129→
   130→**Detailed outline:** `/docs/curriculum/assembly/nintendo-nes/game-04-full-game-outline-nes-pellet-panic.md`
   131→
   132→---
   133→
   134→#### Game 5: Crypt Crawler
   135→**Units:** 128 (8 phases)
   136→**Concept:** Flip-screen top-down dungeon exploration polished to commercial quality. Navigate interconnected rooms, collect keys and treasures, defeat monsters, find the exit. The ancestor to Zelda-style adventure games.
   137→
   138→**Skills taught:**
   139→- Room data structures and metatile system
   140→- Screen transitions with PPU updates
   141→- Persistent state across rooms

   553→---
   554→
   555→## Platform Context
   556→
   557→### Hardware
   558→
   559→- **CPU:** Ricoh 2A03 (MOS 6502 core) @ 1.79 MHz (NTSC) / 1.66 MHz (PAL)
   560→- **RAM:** 2KB work RAM, 2KB video RAM
   561→- **Display:** 256×240 pixels (NTSC), 256×224 visible
   562→- **Colours:** 52 colours in palette, 25 on-screen (4 background palettes + 4 sprite palettes, 4 colours each including shared background)
   563→- **Sound:** APU - 2 pulse, 1 triangle, 1 noise, 1 DMC (sample) channel
   564→- **Storage:** Cartridge with ROM, optional battery backup
   565→
   566→### The PPU (Picture Processing Unit)
   567→
   568→The heart of NES graphics:
   569→
   570→- **Pattern Tables** - Two 4KB tables holding 8×8 pixel tile definitions (256 tiles each for background and sprites)
   571→- **Nametables** - Four 1KB screens defining which tiles appear where (32×30 tiles visible)
   572→- **Attribute Tables** - Define which palette each 16×16 pixel area uses
   573→- **OAM (Object Attribute Memory)** - 256 bytes defining 64 sprites (position, tile, palette, flip)
   574→
   575→### The 8-Sprite Limit
   576→
   577→Only 8 sprites can appear on a single scanline. More causes flickering. Managing this limit is a core NES skill.
   578→
   579→### The APU (Audio Processing Unit)
   580→
   581→Five channels of distinctive 8-bit sound:
   582→- **Pulse 1 & 2** - Square waves with duty cycle control (melodies, harmony)
   583→- **Triangle** - Pure triangle wave (bass, smooth tones)
   584→- **Noise** - White noise generator (drums, percussion, effects)
   585→- **DMC** - Delta modulation for samples (voice, complex sounds)
   586→
   587→### Mappers
   588→
   589→Bank-switching hardware on cartridges enabling larger games:
   590→- **NROM** - No mapper, 32KB PRG + 8KB CHR (early games)
   591→- **MMC1** - Bank switching up to 256KB PRG (Zelda, Metroid)
   592→- **MMC3** - Advanced features, scanline counter (Super Mario Bros. 3)
   593→
   594→The curriculum progresses from NROM-compatible basics to MMC3 mastery.
   595→

1→# Commodore Amiga 68000 Assembly Curriculum
     2→
     3→**Platform:** Commodore Amiga
     4→**Language:** 68000 Assembly
     5→**Prerequisite:** AMOS BASIC curriculum (recommended) or equivalent experience
     6→**Games:** 16 (OCS/ECS) + 8 AGA enhancements
     7→**Total Units:** 4,032 (3,584 OCS/ECS + 448 AGA)
     8→
     9→---
    10→
    11→## Overview
    12→
    13→The Amiga 68000 Assembly curriculum teaches direct hardware programming through 16 complete games. The sequence begins with the custom chipset fundamentals - Agnus, Denise, and Paula working together - and progresses through Blitter mastery, Copper programming, parallax scrolling, isometric projection, and advanced techniques until learners can produce commercial-quality games.
    14→
    15→The Amiga wasn't about one killer feature. It was about the custom chips working in harmony: the Blitter accelerating graphics, the Copper synchronising effects to the display beam, Paula playing four channels of sampled audio. This curriculum teaches learners to conduct that orchestra.
    16→
    17→This is an expanded curriculum because the Amiga's hardware requires deeper coverage - Blitter operations, Copper lists, DMA timing, and the interplay between all components.
    18→
    19→By the final game, learners will have skills matching professional Amiga developers of the early 1990s.

    46→## Game Sequence
    47→
    48→### Foundation Games
    49→
    50→#### Game 1: Exodus
    51→**Units:** 128 (8 phases)
    52→**Concept:** Terrain puzzle in the Lemmings tradition. Creatures walk across a bitmap landscape — the player modifies the terrain to guide them to safety. Digging, building, and redirecting are all Blitter operations. The Amiga's custom chipset becomes the game design tool.
    53→
    54→**Skills taught:**
    55→- 68000 assembly fundamentals
    56→- Display setup and Copper list basics
    57→- Bitplane graphics and bitmap terrain
    58→- Blitter operations (copy, fill, clear, modify)
    59→- Terrain as modifiable bitmap data
    60→- Copper gradients and colour effects (sky, ground)
    61→- Paula sample playback (digging, building, arrival sounds)
    62→- Bootable ADF creation (from Unit 1)
    63→- Creature AI (walking, turning at walls, falling)
    64→- Terrain modification (digging tunnels, building bridges)
    65→- Player input for assigning abilities
    66→- Collision detection against bitmap terrain
    67→- UI panel with Copper screen split
    68→- Level data structures and progression
    69→
    70→**Why first:** A terrain puzzle makes the Blitter the core mechanic from Unit 1. Digging a tunnel is a Blitter clear. Building a bridge is a Blitter copy. Every game action is a hardware operation — learners see the chipset working because it IS the gameplay. The Copper splits the screen for the UI panel, Paula plays satisfying terrain sounds, and the 68000 orchestrates everything. Bootable ADF from day one.
    71→
    72→**Detailed outline:** `/docs/curriculum/assembly/commodore-amiga/game-01-full-game-outline-amiga-exodus.md`

   510→
   511→## AGA Enhancement Games
   512→
   513→The AGA (Advanced Graphics Architecture) chipset, introduced with the A1200 and A4000 in 1992, offered significant improvements over OCS/ECS. Many commercial games shipped in separate OCS and AGA versions - learners follow this industry practice by creating enhanced AGA versions of Games 9-16.
   514→
   515→### AGA Curriculum Structure
   516→
   517→| OCS Game | OCS Units | AGA Enhancement | AGA Units |
   518→|----------|-----------|-----------------|-----------|
   519→| 9: Parallax | 128 | Parallax AGA | 32 |
   520→| 10: Wanderer | 128 | Wanderer AGA | 32 |
   521→| 11: Brawler | 128 | Brawler AGA | 32 |
   522→| 12: Highway | 128 | Highway AGA | 32 |
   523→| 13: Onslaught | 128 | Onslaught AGA | 32 |
   524→| 14: Fortress | 128 | Fortress AGA | 32 |
   525→| 15: Echoes | 256 | Echoes AGA | 64 |
   526→| 16: Ascension | 512 | Ascension AGA | 128 |
   527→| **Totals** | **1,536** | | **384** |
   528→
   529→### AGA Hardware Features
   530→
   531→**Lisa (replaces Denise):**
   532→- 256 colours on-screen from 16.7 million (24-bit) palette
   533→- 8 bitplane modes (vs 6 for ECS)
   534→- Enhanced sprite capabilities
   535→- Wider sprites (64 pixels)
   536→
   537→**Alice (replaces Agnus):**
   538→- 2MB chip RAM support
   539→- Enhanced DMA bandwidth
   540→- Improved Blitter
   541→
   542→**Key AGA Modes:**
   543→- 256-colour chunky/planar modes
   544→- HAM8 - 262,144 colours (vs HAM6's 4,096)
   545→- Super hi-res with more colours
   546→
   547→### AGA Enhancement Games (32-128 Units Each)
   548→

   551→**Enhancement focus:** 256-colour parallax layers, smoother gradients, enhanced palette cycling.

   576→

   578→
   579→#### Game 14 AGA: Fortress AGA
   580→**Units:** 32 (2 phases)
   581→**Enhancement focus:** Richer isometric tiles, enhanced lighting effects, more detailed environments.
   582→
   583→---
   584→
   585→#### Game 15 AGA: Echoes of the Ancients AGA
   586→**Units:** 128 (8 phases)
   587→**Enhancement focus:** 256-colour Metroidvania visuals with enhanced region palettes, smoother parallax with additional layers, HAM8 cutscenes for story moments, enhanced atmospheric effects, richer boss encounters.
   588→
   589→---
   590→
   591→#### Game 16 AGA: Ascension AGA
   592→**Units:** 128 (8 phases)
   593→**Enhancement focus:** Full AGA showcase - 256-colour graphics throughout, HAM8 cinematics, enhanced effects, A1200/A4000 optimisations. The definitive version.
   594→
   595→---
   596→
   597→### AGA Skills Taught
   598→
   599→| Phase | Skills |
   600→|-------|--------|
   601→| Games 9-12 AGA | AGA register basics, 256-colour modes, palette expansion |
   602→| Games 13-14 AGA | Enhanced bitplane modes, AGA Copper techniques |
   603→| Game 15 AGA | HAM8 mode, advanced AGA effects |
   604→| Game 16 AGA | Full AGA mastery, A1200/A4000 optimisation |
   605→
   606→---
   607→
   608→## Hardware Skill Progression
   609→
   610→### Blitter Progression
   611→
   612→| Games | Blitter Skills |
   613→|-------|---------------|
   614→| 1-4 | Terrain modification, basic copy, simple BOBs |
   615→| 5-8 | Cookie-cut, fills, tile rendering |
   616→| 9-11 | Scrolling updates, large BOBs |
   617→| 12-14 | Line draw, complex fills, isometric |
   618→| 15 | Optimised blits, interleaved |
   619→| 16 | Full Blitter mastery |
   620→
   621→### Copper Progression
   622→
   623→| Games | Copper Skills |
   624→|-------|--------------|
   625→| 1-4 | Display setup, colour splits |
   626→| 5-8 | Palette changes, transitions |
   627→| 9-11 | Parallax coordination, gradients |
   628→| 12-14 | Road effects, sprite repositioning |
   629→| 15 | Complex display lists |
   630→| 16 | Full Copper mastery |
   631→
   632→### Paula Progression
   633→
   634→| Games | Audio Skills |
   635→|-------|-------------|
   636→| 1-4 | Sample playback, sound effects |
   637→| 5-8 | Multiple samples, mixing basics |
   638→| 9-11 | MOD replay, dynamic music |
   639→| 12-14 | Positional audio, effects |
   640→| 15 | Advanced mixing, priorities |
   641→| 16 | Full audio production |
   642→
   643→---
   644→
   645→## Platform Context
   646→
   647→### Hardware
   648→
   649→- **CPU:** Motorola 68000 @ 7.16 MHz (NTSC) / 7.09 MHz (PAL)
   650→- **RAM:** 512KB chip RAM (A500), expandable
   651→- **Display:** Multiple modes, typically 320×256 (PAL) or 320×200 (NTSC)
   652→- **Colours:** 32 on-screen from 4096 palette (OCS/ECS), more with tricks
   653→- **Sound:** Paula - 4 channels, 8-bit PCM samples
   654→- **Storage:** 880KB 3.5" floppy disks
   655→
   656→### The Custom Chipset
   657→
   658→**Agnus** - DMA controller and Blitter
   659→- Coordinates all memory access
   660→- Contains the Blitter for fast graphics
   661→- Contains the Copper coprocessor
   662→- Manages bitplane, sprite, audio DMA
   663→
   664→**Denise** - Display and sprites
   665→- Generates video output
   666→- Manages 8 hardware sprites
   667→- Handles playfield display
   668→- Colour palette registers
   669→
   670→**Paula** - Audio and disk
   671→- Four channels of sampled sound
   672→- Floppy disk control
   673→- Interrupt control
   674→
   675→### Key Concepts
   676→
   677→**Copper:** Display-list coprocessor that executes commands synchronised to the video beam. Essential for:
   678→- Palette changes mid-screen
   679→- Sprite repositioning
   680→- Register modifications
   681→- Parallax effects
   682→
   683→**Blitter:** Hardware block transfer and drawing:
   684→- Area copy with masking
   685→- Line drawing
   686→- Area filling
   687→- Cookie-cut compositing
   688→
   689→**Hardware Sprites:** 8 sprites, 16 pixels wide, 3 colours + transparent. Can be:
   690→- Paired for 15 colours
   691→- Repositioned via Copper for more appearances
   692→- Attached for wider sprites
   693→
   694→**Blitter Objects (BOBs):** Software sprites drawn by Blitter:
   695→- Any size
   696→- Any colour depth
   697→- More flexible but CPU/Blitter intensive
   698→
   699→---
   700→
   701→## Distribution
   702→
   703→Games are distributed as:
   704→- ADF disk images (880KB)
   705→- Multi-disk for larger games
   706→- Bootable (game loads directly)
   707→- Optional AmigaDOS loading for development
   708→
   709→---
   710→
   711→## Era Comparison
   712→
   713→| Games | Era Equivalent | Commercial Examples |
   714→|-------|----------------|---------------------|
   715→| 1-4 | 1985-1986 | Marble Madness, Defender of the Crown |
   716→| 5-8 | 1987-1988 | Barbarian, Xenon |
   717→| 9-11 | 1988-1990 | Shadow of the Beast, Turrican |
   718→| 12-14 | 1990-1991 | Lotus, SWIV, Cadaver |
   719→| 15 | 1991-1992 | Super Metroid-style exploration |
   720→| 16 | 1992-1994 | Lionheart, The Chaos Engine |
   721→
   722→By Game 16, learners produce work comparable to late-era commercial releases.
   723→
   724→---
   725→
   726→## Version History
   727→
   728→- **3.18 (2026-02-06):** Replaced Game 1 Blast Zone (shooter) with Exodus (terrain puzzle) to showcase the Blitter as a game mechanic. Lemmings-style terrain modification is a natural fit for Amiga hardware.
   729→- **3.17 (2026-01-18):** Expanded Game 15 (Echoes of the Ancients) to 512 units (OCS/ECS) and 128 units (AGA) with full Metroidvania and per-region parallax. OCS/ECS total now 3,584 units, AGA total now 448 units, grand total 4,032 units.
   730→- **3.16 (2026-01-18):** Expanded Game 14 (Fortress) to 256 units with Blitter-based isometric adventure. OCS/ECS total now 3,328 units.
   731→- **3.15 (2026-01-18):** Expanded Game 13 (Onslaught) to 256 units with bullet patterns and rank system. OCS/ECS total now 3,200 units.
   732→- **3.14 (2026-01-18):** Expanded Game 12 (Highway) to 256 units with pseudo-3D racing and Copper road rendering. OCS/ECS total now 3,072 units.
   733→- **3.13 (2026-01-18):** Expanded Game 11 (Brawler) to 256 units with Y-depth sorting. OCS/ECS total now 2,944 units.
   734→- **3.12 (2026-01-18):** Expanded Game 10 (Wanderer) to 256 units with full platformer worlds. OCS/ECS total now 2,816 units.
   735→- **3.11 (2026-01-18):** Expanded Game 9 (Parallax) to 256 units with dual playfield mastery and multi-layer parallax. OCS/ECS total now 2,688 units.
   736→- **3.10 (2026-01-18):** Expanded Game 8 (Tetrad) to 128 units with Blitter line clearing, T-spins, and competitive multiplayer. OCS/ECS total now 2,560 units.
   737→- **3.9 (2026-01-18):** Expanded Game 7 (Venom) to 128 units with ring buffer, two-player split-screen, and theme system. OCS/ECS total now 2,496 units.
   738→- **3.8 (2026-01-18):** Expanded Game 6 (Gobble) to 128 units with four ghost AI personalities. OCS/ECS total now 2,432 units.
   739→- **3.7 (2026-01-18):** Expanded Game 5 (Crypt) to 128 units with full dungeon exploration. OCS/ECS total now 2,368 units.
   740→- **3.6 (2026-01-18):** Expanded Game 4 (Ascent) to 128 units with full platformer polish. OCS/ECS total now 2,304 units.
   741→- **3.5 (2026-01-18):** Expanded Game 3 (Shatter Point) to 128 units with Blitter physics and power-ups. OCS/ECS total now 2,240 units.
   742→- **3.4 (2026-01-18):** Expanded Game 2 (Signal) to 128 units for full timing-game polish. OCS/ECS total now 2,176 units.
   743→- **3.3 (2026-01-18):** Restructured Game 1. Blast Zone (shooter) as first game at 128 units for immediate engagement, bootable from Unit 1. Signal moved to Game 2. (Blast Zone later replaced by Exodus in 3.18.)
   744→- **3.2 (2026-01-07):** Added AGA enhancement games (384 units) for Games 9-16. Total now 2,432 units.
   745→- **3.1 (2026-01-07):** Corrected structure to use only powers of 2 for unit counts (64/128/256/512).
   746→- **3.0 (2025-01-07):** Restructured to 2,048 units with expanded hardware coverage. AMOS moved to separate curriculum.
   747→- **2.0 (2025-11-26):** Complete rewrite. Replaced phases/tiers/lessons with games model.
   748→- **1.0 (2025-01-15):** Original curriculum.
   749→

content.text:
Excellent. I've now read all five core documentation files. Let me provide you with a comprehensive analysis of the curriculum.

## Summary: Code Like It's 198x - All 64 Games Across Four Platforms

**Total:** 64 games × 3,584 units each = 229,376 units across the four main platforms (Spectrum, C64, NES, Amiga)

---

## ZX Spectrum (16 games, 3,584 units)

### Killer Feature: Attribute colour system

**Games 1-8 (128 units each):** Foundation
1. **Shadowkeep** — Top-down maze explorer. Walk rooms, collect keys, avoid creatures. Attribute system is the game mechanic (INK = walls, PAPER = floors, BRIGHT = items).
2. **Shatter** — Breakout with physics, power-ups, 20 levels. Sub-cell movement and ball bounce angles.
3. **Cavern** — Single-screen platformer (Manic Miner tradition). Gravity, jump physics, moving platforms, air supply timer.
4. **Chambers** — Flip-screen top-down Atic Atac-style exploration. Multi-room, inventory, keys, locked doors, multiple enemy types.
5. **Ink War** — Territory control strategy (unique to Spectrum). AI with minimax, multiple game modes, turn-based.
6. **Chomp** — Pac-Man with four distinct ghost personalities (Blinky chase, Pinky ambush, Inky flank, Clyde random). Full implementation with Cruise Elroy and intermission screens.
7. **Slither** — Snake game. Ring buffer data structure, self-collision, power-ups, AI opponent, wrap-around mode.
8. **Stack** — Tetris with T-spins, wall kicks, SRS rotation, competitive multiplayer, 7-bag randomiser.

**Games 9-10 (256 units each):** Scrolling mastery
9. **Blitz** — R-Type style horizontal shooter. **THE hard Spectrum technique: software pixel scrolling**. Contended memory timing, character and pixel-level shifts, parallax, 5 levels, 5 bosses, weapon upgrades.
10. **Expanse** — Epic scrolling platformer. Scrolling + platformer physics combined. Four themed worlds, bosses, checkpoints, 256 units for commercial polish.

**Games 11-13 (256 units each):** Complex systems
11. **Redline** — Vertical scrolling racer. AI opponents, rubber banding, weather, pit stops. **Introduces tape loading and TAP file creation** - standard loader fundamentals.
12. **Recoil** — Contra-style run 'n' gun. 8-way shooting, 5 weapon types, 5 stages, 5 bosses, two-player co-op.
13. **Knuckle Brawl** — Streets of Rage/Final Fight beat 'em up. Y-depth sorting (bridge to isometric), combo systems, 5 stages, 5 bosses, two-player co-op. **Bridge to isometric paradigm.**

**Games 14-16 (256/512/512 units):** Advanced paradigms
14. **Strafe** — 1942-style vertical shmup. Formation patterns, bullet patterns, ground/air targets, 7 stages, multi-phase bosses. **Introduces 128K detection, AY-3-8912 sound, bank switching** - first 128K game.
15. **Bastion** — Knight Lore-style isometric adventure (512 units). Transformation curse mechanic (human/beast forms with timer). 100+ rooms across 5 zones, isometric mathematics, depth sorting, push-block puzzles, multiple endings.
16. **Spectral Legacy** — Capstone (512 units). 5-zone action-adventure combining all techniques. **128K/48K dual support, turbo tape loader, TZX file creation, +3 disk support.** Password systems, commercial quality.

**Optional BASIC Gateway:** 8 games × 64 units (512 total) for learners wanting pre-assembly fundamentals. Completely optional — assembly curriculum is self-contained.

---

## Commodore 64 (16 games, 3,584 units)

### Killer Feature: SID sound chip

**Games 1-8 (128 units each):** Foundation
1. **Starfield** — Single-screen space shooter. Immediate engagement: ship on screen by Unit 2, shooting by Unit 3. Sprites, collision, wave systems, power-ups, SID sound effects throughout.
2. **Platform Panic** — Single-screen platformer. Gravity, jump physics, collision, animation, ladders, full SID music.
3. **Maze Raider** — Pac-Man style. Custom character graphics, ghost AI (4 personalities with specific behaviours), pathfinding, state machines, SID audio.
4. **Brick Basher** — Breakout. Fixed-point physics, paddle angle control, brick types, power-ups, 20 levels. Full physics polish.
5. **Catacombs** — Flip-screen dungeon exploration. Room transitions, inventory, combat, locked doors, multiple enemy types, boss battles.
6. **SID Symphony** — Rhythm game (killer feature deep dive). Notes scroll on three tracks (one per SID voice). **Deep SID mastery:** all three voices, ADSR, waveforms, filter control, resonance, ring modulation, sync, PWM, timing accuracy (Perfect/Good/OK), combo systems, song editor.
7. **Scroll Runner** — Endless horizontal scroller. **VIC-II hardware scrolling ($D016), double buffering, raster interrupts, colour RAM sync.** Parallax, sprite multiplexing for extra enemies.
8. **Night Raid** — Scramble-style horizontal shooter. Scrolling + shooting combined, resource management (fuel), dual weapons (shots + bombs), ground and air targets, boss encounters, parallax starfield, 2-player mode, turbo loader.

**Games 9-10 (256 units each):** Advanced hardware
9. **Sprite Storm** — Arena shooter (256 units). **THE C64 technique: sprite multiplexing (8→32 sprites). Y-sorted allocation, raster interrupt timing, double IRQ, cycle-exact code, flicker management, crunch bug avoidance.** Games like Katakis/Turrican relied on this.
10. **Raster Rider** — Outrun-style pseudo-3D (256 units). Per-scanline colour manipulation, perspective road mathematics, sprite scaling, 5 themed tracks, championship mode, ghost replay, SID engine synthesis.

**Games 11-13 (256 units each):** Complex systems
11. **Dungeon Crawl** — Zelda-style adventure (256 units). Multi-room, inventory, NPCs, quests, melee+ranged combat, puzzles. **Introduces Kernal disk I/O routines, disk save/load system, D64 disk image creation.** Password backup for non-disk users.
12. **Arena Fighter** — Street Fighter II-inspired (256 units). 4 unique characters, special moves, combos, juggle mechanics, motion input detection (QCF/QCB/DP motions), AI opponent with spacing awareness, tournament mode.
13. **Parallax Patrol** — Gradius-style shmup (256 units). Multi-layer parallax (4+ speed layers), option drones (position history buffer), charge shots (3 levels), 4 weapon types, 7 stages, epic bosses, rank system, 2-player co-op.

**Games 14-16 (256/512/512 units):** Advanced paradigms
14. **Isometric Quest** — Knight Lore-style (256 units). Isometric projection mathematics, depth sorting, 5 zones with unique themes, push-block puzzles, combat, multiple endings.
15. **Mega Blaster** — Turrican-style run 'n' gun (512 units). 8-way scrolling, advanced weapon system with upgrade paths, grappling hook physics (rope simulation with sine table), vehicle transformation (wheel mode with different physics), 6 worlds × 4 stages, massive multi-phase bosses, exploration with secrets, environmental hazards.
16. **Symphony's End** — Capstone (512 units). All techniques at mastery. **Demo-scene effects: FLD, DYCP, AGSP, tech-tech, raster bars. Advanced SID: filters, ring modulation, multi-speed, digi-drums. Direct 1541 drive programming (drive's 6502, serial protocol), custom fast loader, IRQ loader with music during load.** 5 zones, multi-phase final boss, combat with combos, inventory, puzzles, story/cutscenes, save/load, 6581/8580 SID detection, dual distribution (D64 + tape).

**Optional BASIC V2 Gateway:** 8 games × 64 units (512 total) for learners wanting pre-assembly fundamentals. Completely optional.

---

## Nintendo NES (16 games, 3,584 units)

### Killer Feature: PPU (tile-based graphics)

**Games 1-8 (128 units each):** Foundation
1. **Dash** — Side-scrolling platformer (NES's natural strength). PPU setup, nametables, OAM sprites, controller input, gravity/jump physics, tile collision, hardware scrolling (PPUSCROLL), camera following, animation states, level data structures.
2. **Crate Escape** — Single-screen platformer. Gravity, jump physics, metatile level design, hazards, moving platforms, ladders, collectibles, boss level.
3. **Fracture Point** — Breakout. Ball physics with fixed-point maths, background tile modification (bricks disappear from nametable), paddle angle, power-ups, APU sound design.
4. **Pellet Panic** — Pac-Man. Tile-based maze, constrained movement, four ghost personalities (chase/scatter/frightened modes), power pills, screen wrap tunnels, demo mode, intermissions.
5. **Crypt Crawler** — Flip-screen dungeon (Zelda ancestor). Room structures, persistent state, inventory (keys, potions), HUD, enemy AI (patrol/chase), sword combat, locked doors, puzzle elements, boss encounters.
6. **Fangs for the Memory** — Snake with vampire bat theme. Ring buffer data structure, grid movement with input buffering, self-collision, food spawning, speed progression, multiple game modes (classic, levels, survival, time attack), 2-player versus.
7. **Gridlock** — Tetris-style. Rotation mathematics (4 orientations), wall kicks, lock delay, grid collision, line detection/clearing, next piece preview, hold piece, ghost piece, T-spin detection, 7-bag randomiser, multiple modes (Marathon/Sprint/Ultra), 2-player versus with garbage.
8. **Pixel Plains** — Scrolling platformer (128 units). Hardware scrolling fundamentals, camera following, level compression, scrolling collision, enemies that spawn/despawn, moving platforms, boss battles, four themed worlds (grass/cave/sky/castle), world map, checkpoints, secret exits, save/password system.

**Games 9-10 (256 units each):** Intermediate
9. **Ion Trail** — Gradius-style shmup (256 units). Horizontal scrolling, Gradius-style power meter system, option drones (position history buffer), multiple weapon types with upgrade levels, charge shot, complex AI/formations, bullet patterns, 5 stages, 5 epic bosses, rank system, sprite management, sprite 0 split for fixed HUD, 2-player co-op, second loop/true ending, battery save.
10. **Arena Assault** — Foundation capstone (256 units). Integration of all foundation skills. 8-way movement/shooting, heavy sprite management, wave-based survival, multiple weapon types, shield/health systems, four bosses, multiple arenas with unique mechanics, 2-player co-op/versus, score attack/endless modes.

**Games 11-14 (256 units each):** Advanced
11. **Thunder Run** — 1942-style vertical shmup (256 units). Vertical scrolling, row-based streaming, formation patterns, bullet patterns, ground/air targets, 5 stages, 5 bosses. **Introduces MMC1 mapper: serial register writing, CHR bank switching, PRG bank switching, multiple tilesets per level.**
12. **Iron Assault** — Contra-style run 'n' gun (256 units). Platformer + shooter hybrid, 8-way shooting with arm rotation, crouching/prone, 6 weapon types (Rifle/Spread/Laser/Machine Gun/Fire/Crush), weapon pickups, bullet pool, enemy variety, large boss assemblies with weak points, 5 stages (Jungle/Base Exterior/Waterfall vertical/Base Interior/Alien Hive), 2-player co-op, sprite limit management with flicker.
13. **Pavement Justice** — Double Dragon/RCR-style beat 'em up (256 units). Y-axis depth sorting with OAM ordering, large metasprite characters, melee combat, combo systems with juggles, attack slots, multiple enemy types, weapon pickups, 5 stages, 5 bosses, 2-player co-op, sprite limit management/flicker.
14. **Tarmac Terror** — Outrun-style racing (256 units). Pseudo-3D road rendering (perspective mathematics), road width/curves/hills, sprite scaling simulation (pre-scaled sprites), player car with lean frames, gear system, curve physics, traffic system, 5 themed tracks (Coastal/City/Desert/Forest/Mountain), branching paths, championship mode, time trial with ghost replay. **Split-screen 2-player with sprite 0 for screen split.** APU engine sound synthesis.

**Games 15-16 (512/512 units):** Expert & Capstone
15. **Echoes of Eponium** — Full Metroidvania (512 units). **MMC3 mapper with IRQ scanline counter, large connected world (5 regions × 40+ rooms).** Non-linear progression with sequence breaking, ability-gating design (8+ abilities unlock areas: wall jump, double jump, dash, morph, charge beam, missiles, bombs), backtracking/shortcuts, 5 region themes, CHR bank switching for varied tilesets, parallax with IRQ timing, battery-backed SRAM saves with multiple slots, 8 major bosses granting abilities, mini-bosses, hidden items, map system with fog of war, percentage tracking, multiple endings, time attack/speedrun modes, advanced APU music with DMC samples, 8+ hours of content.
16. **Requiem for a Mapper** — Capstone (512 units). All techniques at mastery, **advanced MMC3 mastery**, large world (5 zones × 16+ rooms), multiple gameplay styles, combat system with sub-weapons, 5 zone bosses + multi-phase final, inventory/equipment, puzzles, story/cutscenes, **battery-backed SRAM saves, NTSC/PAL compatibility, optional expansion audio (VRC6/MMC5), ROM image creation, physical cartridge production (PCB/shell/manual).** Professional presentation.

---

## Commodore Amiga (16 games OCS/ECS + 8 AGA enhancements = 4,032 total units)

### Killer Feature: Custom chipset (Agnus, Denise, Paula working in harmony)

**Games 1-8 (128 units each, OCS/ECS):** Foundation
1. **Exodus** — Terrain puzzle (Lemmings tradition, 128 units). Creatures walk on bitmap terrain; player modifies terrain using Blitter operations (digging tunnels = Blitter clear, building bridges = Blitter copy). **The Blitter becomes the game mechanic.** Copper gradients for sky/ground, Paula sample playback for digging sounds, Copper screen split for UI panel, creature AI, bootable ADF from Unit 1.
2. **Signal** — Frogger-style lane crosser (128 units). Grid-based hop movement, lane objects as BOBs (cars, logs, turtles), riding moving objects (velocity transfer), timing-based gameplay, Copper gradients, MOD music with SFX, 15 levels, multiple hazards.
3. **Shatter Point** — Breakout (128 units). Ball physics with 16.16 fixed-point maths, Blitter fills for brick rendering/destruction, Copper gradient backgrounds, Paula sample playback for varied impacts, paddle angle, multiple brick types, 20 levels, MOD music.
4. **Ascent** — Single-screen platformer (128 units). Gravity/jump physics, BOB animation (walk/jump frames), Blitter cookie-cut masking, moving platforms, crumbling platforms, patrolling/flying enemies, power-ups, 20 levels across themed worlds, boss battles, MOD music.
5. **Crypt** — Flip-screen dungeon (128 units). Room structures with Blitter tile rendering, screen transitions with Copper fades, persistent state, inventory (keys/potions/weapons), enemy AI (patrol/chase/ranged), combat system, locked doors, push blocks, multiple dungeon floors, Copper zone palettes, boss encounters, save system.
6. **Gobble** — Pac-Man (128 units). Tile-based maze with Blitter rendering, constrained movement, four ghost AI personalities (Blinky/Pinky/Inky/Clyde), chase/scatter/frightened modes, screen wrapping, power pills, difficulty progression, Copper backgrounds, Paula samples (waka/siren), MOD title music, demo mode, 2-player simultaneous.
7. **Venom** — Snake (128 units). Ring buffer implementation in 68000, grid movement, self-collision detection, Blitter-based segment rendering, growth mechanics, speed scaling, multiple level packs with mazes, moving obstacles, teleport portals, power-ups (slow-down, ghost, multiplier), 2-player split-screen, AI opponent, theme system (Jungle/Tech/Desert), Copper effects, Paula SFX + module music.
8. **Tetrad** — Tetris (128 units). Rotation mathematics in 68000, grid collision detection, **line clearing with Blitter scroll operations**, efficient Blitter-based grid rendering, next/hold piece, ghost projection, wall kicks, T-spin detection, 7-bag randomiser, speed progression (20 levels), multiple modes (Marathon/Sprint/Ultra/20G), 2-player split-screen versus with garbage, AI opponent, theme system, Copper effects, Paula SFX + module music.

**Games 9-10 (256 units each, OCS/ECS):** Intermediate
9. **Parallax** — Multi-layer parallax shooter (256 units). **THE Amiga showcase technique: dual playfield mode (DBLPF in BPLCON0).** Multi-layer parallax (4+ speed layers), bitplane DMA timing mastery, hardware scroll registers (BPLCON1), **Copper list programming for per-line effects**, raster bars, colour cycling, Blitter buffer management, water reflections, vertical parallax, 5 levels, 5 multi-phase bosses, weapon upgrades with options, Paula MOD replay, A1200 AGA enhanced version. **Shadow of the Beast defined the platform with these effects.**
10. **Wanderer** — Scrolling platformer (256 units). Horizontal scrolling platformer, camera systems with dead zones, level compression/streaming, slope physics, moving platforms, enemy variety, combat system (stomp/projectile), four themed worlds (Grasslands/Underground/Sky/Castle), bosses per world, multi-layer parallax per world, Copper colour effects, weather effects (rain/snow), world map navigation, save system, time attack/boss rush, AGA enhanced version.

**Games 11-13 (256 units each, OCS/ECS):** Advanced
11. **Brawler** — Streets of Rage/Final Fight beat 'em up (256 units). Y-axis depth sorting algorithms, large sprite animation (32×48+), melee combat with hit detection, combo systems/chains, juggle mechanics, special moves with meter, grab/throw mechanics, enemy AI coordination, attack slot system, multiple enemy types, weapon pickups (pipe, knife), 4 levels, 4 bosses, 2-player cooperative, versus mode, character selection with stats, destructible objects.
12. **Highway** — Outrun-style racing (256 units). Perspective projection mathematics, **Copper-based road rendering (per-scanline colours)**, road width/curves/hills calculation, horizon line/ground plane, sprite scaling simulation for depth, pre-scaled sprite system, player car with lean frames, steering/acceleration/braking, gear system, curve physics (centrifugal force), traffic system, 5 themed tracks, championship/time trial modes, split-screen 2-player with Copper, ghost replay, Paula engine synthesis, MOD music, AGA enhanced version.
13. **Onslaught** — 1942/SWIV-style vertical shmup (256 units). Vertical scrolling, formation patterns (V/line/circle/spiral), bullet patterns (aimed/spread/ring/spiral), ground/air targets with varied behaviours, multi-phase bosses with weak points, weapon upgrade system (Wide/Laser/Spread/Homing), missiles/bombs, options/satellites, 5 themed stages, rank system (adaptive difficulty), 2-player cooperative, grazing bonus, Paula MOD soundtrack, AGA enhanced version.
14. **Fortress** — Knight Lore/Cadaver isometric (256 units). Isometric projection mathematics, world-to-screen conversion, depth sorting (painter's algorithm), **Blitter-based isometric tile rendering**, Z-height collision, push block puzzles, pressure plates, doors/gates, combat system (sword/shield/magic), multiple enemy types (Skeleton/Ghost/Knight), 5 zones with unique themes (Entrance/Dungeons/Tower/Crypt/Throne), Copper colour effects per zone, 5 zone bosses + multi-phase final, inventory/equipment, magic system (Fireball/Shield/Teleport) with mana, disk save system, MOD music + Paula SFX, good/best ending, New Game+, AGA enhanced version.

**Games 15-16 (512/512 units, OCS/ECS):** Expert & Capstone
15. **Echoes of the Ancients** — Full Metroidvania (512 units OCS/ECS). Large connected world (5 regions × 50+ rooms), non-linear progression with sequence breaking, ability-gating design (10+ abilities), movement abilities (wall jump, double jump, dash, morph, grapple), combat abilities (charge beam, spread shot, missiles, bombs), backtracking/shortcuts, region-specific visual themes with unique palettes, multi-layer parallax per region, Copper colour effects per zone (sky gradients, water, fire), advanced Blitter techniques, disk-based saves with multiple slots, 10 major bosses granting abilities, mini-bosses, hidden items/expansions, map system with fog of war, percentage completion, multiple endings, time attack/speedrun, full MOD soundtrack with region themes, Paula samples, memory management for large games, multi-disk support, **AGA enhanced version with 256 colours and HAM8 cutscenes** (128 units AGA).
16. **Ascension: The Guru Meditation** — Capstone (512 units OCS/ECS). All previous techniques at mastery, large world design, multiple gameplay styles, deep combat system, equipment/magic, NPC dialogue/quests, full save system, advanced Copper tricks, optimised Blitter usage, custom disk loading, professional production values, complete portfolio piece. **AGA enhanced version** (128 units) — full AGA showcase with 256-colour graphics, HAM8 cinematics, enhanced effects, A1200/A4000 optimisation. Title reclaims the infamous "Guru Meditation" Amiga error.

### AGA Enhancement Games (384 units total)
Games 9-16 OCS/ECS versions have optional AGA versions (8 games):
- Games 9-14 AGA: 32 units each (128 total) — 256-colour modes, enhanced palettes/sprites/effects
- Game 15 AGA: 64 units — HAM8 cutscenes, enhanced atmospheric effects
- Game 16 AGA: 128 units — Full AGA showcase, A1200/A4000 optimisation

---

## Analysis: Which Games Are Genuinely Exciting?

### Definitely exciting (inspired by/reference real commercial games):
- **Spectrum Blitz** (R-Type style) — Software scrolling is genuinely hard and impressive
- **Spectrum Bastion/Spectral Legacy** (Knight Lore style isometric)
- **C64 Sprite Storm** (Katakis/Turrican reference) — Sprite multiplexing is *the* C64 technique
- **C64 Mega Blaster** (Turrican-style) — Grappling hook physics, vehicle transformation
- **C64 Parallax Patrol** (Gradius) — Multi-layer parallax with options
- **NES Ion Trail** (Gradius) — Power-up system, options, danmaku-lite
- **NES Echoes of Eponium** (Metroid style) — Full Metroidvania with ability gating
- **Amiga Parallax** (Shadow of the Beast reference) — Dual playfield parallax as definitive Amiga showcase
- **Amiga Echoes of the Ancients** (Metroid-style Metroidvania)
- **Amiga Highway** (Outrun style) — Copper-based road rendering, perspective maths

### Clever, unique concepts:
- **Spectrum Ink War** — Territory control strategy using the attribute system as core mechanic (unique to Spectrum)
- **Spectrum Redline** — Racing game that introduces tape loading fundamentals
- **C64 SID Symphony** — Rhythm game that is a deep dive into the SID chip
- **Amiga Exodus** — Terrain puzzle (Lemmings-style) where Blitter operations ARE the game mechanic
- **Amiga Signal** — Frogger with Amiga audiovisual focus

### Teaching exercises (solid but less distinctive):
- **Shadowkeep, Chambers, Catacombs, Crypt Crawler** — Multi-room exploration games (useful but formulaic)
- **Shatter, Shatter Point, Fracture Point** — Breakout clones (physics teaching, repetitive concept)
- **Platform Panic, Crate Escape, Ascent, Cavern** — Single-screen platformers (foundational but similar)
- **Maze Raider, Gobble, Chomp, Pellet Panic** — Pac-Man variants (four similar games across platforms)
- **Slither, Venom, Fangs for the Memory** — Snake games (ring buffer teaching, repetitive)
- **Stack, Gridlock, Tetrad** — Tetris clones (rotation/grid teaching, repetitive)

---

## Difficulty Progression Within Each Platform

### Spectrum progression (most detailed):
**Games 1-8:** Single-screen fundamentals (attribute system mastery, physics, AI)
**Games 9-10:** Software scrolling introduction (hardest technique on the platform)
**Games 11-13:** Complex multi-system games (racing, run 'n' gun, beat 'em up)
**Games 14-15:** Advanced paradigms (vertical shmup with 128K/AY, then isometric Knight Lore-style)
**Game 16:** Commercial capstone (128K/48K dual support, turbo loaders, +3 disk)

### C64 progression (deepest hardware mastery):
**Games 1-8:** Single-screen/basic scrolling (VIC-II sprites, CIA input, SID sound)
**Games 9-10:** Advanced VIC-II (sprite multiplexing, raster timing, pseudo-3D)
**Games 11-13:** Complex systems (disk I/O, fighting game frame data, parallax)
**Game 14:** Isometric paradigm shift (Knight Lore-style)
**Game 15:** Turrican-style run 'n' gun (8-way scrolling, grappling physics, vehicle transformation)
**Game 16:** Demo-scene mastery (FLD, DYCP, AGSP, fast loaders, drive programming)

### NES progression (mapper mastery):
**Games 1-8:** PPU/APU fundamentals (nametables, collision, scrolling basics - NROM only, 32KB PRG)
**Games 9-10:** Foundation capstone (Gradius-style, arena shooter - still NROM)
**Games 11-14:** Mapper progression (MMC1 bank switching, vertical shmup, run 'n' gun, beat 'em up, pseudo-3D racing)
**Game 15:** Metroidvania (MMC3 with scanline counter, SRAM saves, complex world design)
**Game 16:** Cartridge mastery capstone (DMC samples, optional expansion audio, physical cartridge production)

### Amiga progression (chipset orchestration):
**Games 1-8:** Blitter/Copper/Paula fundamentals (terrain puzzle as hook, timing game, physics, platformer, dungeon, maze, snake, puzzle)
**Games 9-10:** Parallel development (dual playfield parallax = showcase technique; platformer combining all)
**Games 11-13:** Specialized systems (beat 'em up Y-sorting, pseudo-3D racing, vertical shmup)
**Game 14:** Isometric paradigm (Knight Lore-style with Blitter)
**Game 15:** Metroidvania (large world, multi-layer parallax per region, ability gating)
**Game 16:** Capstone (all techniques, AGA enhanced version as showcase)

---

## Real Commercial Game References

**Spectrum:**
- Manic Miner (Cavern)
- Atic Atac (Chambers)
- Pac-Man (Chomp)
- R-Type (Blitz)
- Head Over Heels (Strafe and Bastion isometric references)
- Midnight Resistance, Navy Moves (Games 9+ era equivalents)

**C64:**
- Pac-Man (Maze Raider)
- Katakis/Turrican (Sprite Storm, Mega Blaster references)
- Gradius (Parallax Patrol)
- Street Fighter II (Arena Fighter)
- System 3/Thalamus games (Game 16 distribution quality target)

**NES:**
- Super Mario Bros. (Dash, platformer focus)
- Metroid (Echoes of Eponium)
- Gradius (Ion Trail)
- Contra (Iron Assault)
- Double Dragon (Pavement Justice)
- Outrun (Tarmac Terror)

**Amiga:**
- Lemmings (Exodus, terrain modification)
- Shadow of the Beast (Parallax - defines the platform)
- Metroid (Echoes of the Ancients)
- Knight Lore/Cadaver (Fortress, isometric)
- Turrican (referenced in design philosophy)
- Outrun (Highway)
- 1942/SWIV (Onslaught)

---

## 64 Games Complete List

**Key Insight:** The curriculum isn't just 64 games — it's 64 progressions from zero assembly knowledge to commercial-quality capability. The "teaching exercise" games (Tetris/Pac-Man/Breakout variants) aren't filler; they're scaffolding. Each platform has the concept appear once for foundational teaching, then learners apply those skills to harder games. The progression is genuinely elegant: platforms start with their killer feature (attributes for Spectrum, SID for C64, PPU for NES, custom chipset for Amiga), then build outward through increasingly complex systems until learners can orchestrate commercial-quality work.
